rule.description: Critical security event: Anomalous behavior or exploit payload detected by system monitoring.

mitre.description: The alert detects an exploitation attempt via a POST request to the /api/v1/auth endpoint. The payload contains a JNDI (Java Naming and Directory Interface) lookup string targeting an external LDAP server (185.220.101.9:1389). The payload includes a Base64 encoded command designed to execute a shell command (curl) to fetch and execute a shell script, which is a characteristic behavior of Log4Shell-style remote code execution (RCE) attacks.
mitre.keywords: T1190, JNDI injection, LDAP exploitation, Remote Code Execution, Log4Shell

cve.description: The log entry shows a malicious JNDI lookup payload (${jndi:ldap://...}) being injected into an HTTP POST request. This pattern is highly indicative of vulnerabilities related to improper input validation in Java-based logging frameworks, specifically targeting vulnerabilities like Log4Shell that allow for remote code execution through LDAP/JNDI injection.
cve.keywords: JNDI injection, LDAP exploit, Log4Shell, RCE

T1059.004.name: Unix Shell
T1059.004.description:
Adversaries may abuse Unix shell commands and scripts for execution. Unix shells are the primary command prompt on Linux, macOS, and ESXi systems, though many variations of the Unix shell exist (e.g. sh, ash, bash, zsh, etc.) depending on the specific OS or distribution.(Citation: DieNet Bash)(Citation: Apple ZShell) Unix shells can control every aspect of a system, with certain commands requiring elevated privileges.

Unix shells also support scripts that enable sequential execution of commands as well as other typical programming operations such as conditionals and loops. Common uses of shell scripts include long or repetitive tasks, or the need to run the same set of commands on multiple systems.

Adversaries may abuse Unix shells to execute various commands or payloads. Interactive shells may be accessed through command and control channels or during lateral movement such as with [SSH](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1021/004). Adversaries may also leverage shell scripts to deliver and execute multiple commands on victims or as part of payloads used for persistence.

Some systems, such as embedded devices, lightweight Linux distributions, and ESXi servers, may leverage stripped-down Unix shells via Busybox, a small executable that contains a variety of tools, including a simple shell.
T1059.004.mitigations: Mitigation 'Execution Prevention': Use application control where appropriate. On ESXi hosts, the `execInstalledOnly` feature prevents binaries from being run unless they have been packaged and signed as part of a vSphere installation bundle (VIB).
T1059.004.detections:
Detection Strategy: Behavioral Detection of Unix Shell Execution
- Analytic 'Analytic 1081': Detects bash, sh, zsh, or BusyBox shell execution initiated via remote sessions, unauthorized users, or embedded within secondary script interpreters. Focus is on chained behavior: shell > suspicious commands > network discovery or persistence indicators.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1082': Identifies use of sh/bash/zsh in suspicious context, such as user scripts launched from non-standard apps (e.g., Preview.app), embedded in LaunchDaemons, or executed outside Terminal.app. Looks for misuse in Automator, LaunchAgents, or NSAppleScript-executed shell.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1083': Detects BusyBox or Ash shell execution from unauthorized logins or remote connections. Focus is on rare shell invocations from DCUI, SSH sessions, or remote management paths. Also watches for payload droppers or persistence artifacts using shell.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1084': Detects Unix shell usage on network appliances (e.g., routers, firewalls, embedded Linux) through rare console commands, CLI interfaces, or script injection via exposed APIs or SSH.
T1059.004.procedures: Software 'BOLDMOVE': BOLDMOVE is capable of spawning a remote command shell. Software 'CASTLETAP': CASTLETAP has the ability to spawn BusyBox command shell in victim environments. Software 'Ebury': Ebury can use the commands `Xcsh` or `Xcls` to open a shell with Ebury level permissions and `Xxsh` to open a shell with root level. Software 'NKAbuse': NKAbuse is initially installed and executed through an initial shell script. Software 'SnappyTCP': SnappyTCP creates the reverse shell using a pthread spawning a bash shell. Software 'Turian': Turian has the ability to use /bin/sh to execute commands. Software 'Bundlore': Bundlore has leveraged /bin/sh and /bin/bash to execute commands on the victim machine. Software 'Penquin': Penquin can execute remote commands using bash scripts. Software 'Kinsing': Kinsing has used Unix shell scripts to execute commands in the victim environment. Software 'Kobalos': Kobalos can spawn a new pseudo-terminal and execute arbitrary commands at the command prompt. Software 'Derusbi': Derusbi is capable of creating a remote Bash shell and executing commands. Software 'PULSECHECK': PULSECHECK can use Unix shell script for command execution. Software 'Chaos': Chaos provides a reverse shell connection on 8338/TCP, encrypted via AES. Software 'COATHANGER': COATHANGER provides a BusyBox reverse shell for command and control. Software 'Drovorub': Drovorub can execute arbitrary commands as root on a compromised system. Software 'OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D': OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D uses a shell script as the main executable inside an app bundle and drops an embedded base64-encoded payload to the /tmp folder. Software 'Anchor': Anchor can execute payloads via shell scripting. Software 'LoudMiner': LoudMiner used shell scripts to launch various services and to start/stop the QEMU virtualization. Software 'Shai-Hulud': Shai-Hulud has utilized Linux shell commands to modify configuration files. Software 'AppleJeus': AppleJeus has used shell scripts to execute commands after installation and set persistence mechanisms. Software 'WindTail': WindTail can use the open command to execute an application. Software 'OSX/Shlayer': OSX/Shlayer can use bash scripts to check the macOS version, download payloads, and extract bytes from files. OSX/Shlayer uses the command sh -c tail -c +1381... to extract bytes at an offset from a specified file. OSX/Shlayer uses the curl -fsL "$url" >$tmp_path command to download malicious payloads into a temporary directory. Software 'NETWIRE': NETWIRE has the ability to use /bin/bash and /bin/sh to execute commands. Software 'Kazuar': Kazuar uses /bin/bash to execute commands on the victim’s machine. Software 'Doki': Doki has executed shell scripts with /bin/sh. Software 'CoinTicker': CoinTicker executes a bash script to establish a reverse shell. Software 'Gomir': Gomir reads command line arguments and parses them for functionality when executed from a Linux shell, and can execute arbitrary strings passed to it as shell commands. Software 'Cuckoo Stealer': Cuckoo Stealer can spawn a bash shell to enable execution on compromised hosts. Software 'ZIPLINE': ZIPLINE can use `/bin/sh` to create a reverse shell and execute commands. Software 'PITSTOP': PITSTOP has the ability to receive shell commands over a Unix domain socket. Software 'VIRTUALPITA': VIRTUALPITA has the ability to spawn a bash shell for script execution. Software 'REPTILE': REPTILE can deploy components automatically with shell scripts. Software 'BPFDoor': BPFDoor can create a reverse shell and supports vt100 emulator formatting. Software 'PACEMAKER': PACEMAKER can use a simple bash script for execution. Software 'Green Lambert': Green Lambert can use shell scripts for execution, such as /bin/sh -c. Software 'Skidmap': Skidmap has used pm.sh to download and install its main payload. Software 'MacMa': MacMa can execute supplied shell commands and uses bash scripts to perform additional actions. Software 'CallMe': CallMe has the capability to create a reverse shell on victims. Software 'Exaramel for Linux': Exaramel for Linux has a command to execute a shell command on the system. Software 'CookieMiner': CookieMiner has used a Unix shell script to run a series of commands targeting macOS. Software 'XCSSET': XCSSET uses a shell script to execute Mach-o files and osacompile commands such as, osacompile -x -o xcode.app main.applescript. Software 'RIFLESPINE': RIFLESPINE can execute commands with `/bin/sh`. Software 'BRICKSTORM': BRICKSTORM has executed shell commands using `/bin/sh`. Software 'J-magic': The J-magic agent is executed through a command line argument which specifies an interface and listening port. Software 'Proton': Proton uses macOS' .command file type to script actions. Software 'Hildegard': Hildegard has used shell scripts for execution. Software 'Fysbis': Fysbis has the ability to create and execute commands in a remote shell for CLI. Group 'Aquatic Panda': Aquatic Panda used malicious shell scripts in Linux environments following access via SSH to install Linux versions of Winnti malware. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has used shell scripts for execution. Group 'Contagious Interview': Contagious Interview has targeted macOS victim hosts using a bash downloader coremedia.sh and a bash script cloud.sh. Group 'Rocke': Rocke used shell scripts to run commands which would obtain persistence and execute the cryptocurrency mining malware. Group 'Velvet Ant': Velvet Ant used a custom tool, VELVETSTING, to parse encoded inbound commands to compromised F5 BIG-IP devices and then execute them via the Unix shell. Group 'Scattered Spider': Scattered Spider has used the command shell to upload and install the Teleport remote access tool to a compromised vCenter Server Appliance. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has used Brightmetricagent.exe which contains a command- line interface (CLI) library that can leverage command shells including Z Shell (zsh).Volt Typhoon used KV Botnet Activity to build intermediate communication chains between operators and victims, such as identified access to victims in Guam.KV Botnet Activity utilizes multiple Bash scripts during botnet installation stages, and the final botnet payload allows for running commands in the Bash shell. Group 'APT41': APT41 used Linux shell commands for system survey and information gathering prior to exploitation of vulnerabilities such as CVE-2019-19871. Group 'UNC3886': UNC3886 has used a bash script to install malicious vSphere Installation Bundles (VIBs).In mid-2024 Mandiant identified custom TINYSHELL-based backdoors deployed on Juniper Networks’ Junos OS routers. Mandiant attributed these backdoors to the China-nexus espionage group UNC3886.During RedPenguin, UNC3886 used malware capable of launching an interactive shell. Group 'Sea Turtle': Sea Turtle used shell scripts for post-exploitation execution in victim environments. Campaign 'Operation MidnightEclipse': During Operation MidnightEclipse, threat actors piped output from stdout to bash for execution. Campaign 'FLORAHOX Activity': FLORAHOX Activity has executed multiple Bash controller scripts to provide command line inputs for FLORAHOX traversal configurations. Campaign 'RedPenguin': During RedPenguin, UNC3886 used malware capable of launching an interactive shell. Campaign '2025 Poland Wiper Attacks': During the 2025 Poland Wiper Attacks, the adversaries utilized the Linux `dd` command to overwrite portions of the disks with random data. Campaign 'KV Botnet Activity': KV Botnet Activity utilizes multiple Bash scripts during botnet installation stages, and the final botnet payload allows for running commands in the Bash shell. Campaign 'Quad7 Activity': Quad7 Activity has enabled the creation of an access-controlled command shell /bin/sh on compromised routers.

T1027.010.name: Command Obfuscation
T1027.010.description: Adversaries may obfuscate content during command execution to impede detection. Command-line obfuscation is a method of making strings and patterns within commands and scripts more difficult to signature and analyze. This type of obfuscation can be included within commands executed by delivered payloads (e.g., [Phishing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1566) and [Drive-by Compromise](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1189)) or interactively via [Command and Scripting Interpreter](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059).(Citation: Akamai JS)(Citation: Malware Monday VBE) For example, adversaries may abuse syntax that utilizes various symbols and escape characters (such as spacing, `^`, `+`. `$`, and `%`) to make commands difficult to analyze while maintaining the same intended functionality.(Citation: RC PowerShell) Many languages support built-in obfuscation in the form of base64 or URL encoding.(Citation: Microsoft PowerShellB64) Adversaries may also manually implement command obfuscation via string splitting (`“Wor”+“d.Application”`), order and casing of characters (`rev <<<'dwssap/cte/ tac'`), globing (`mkdir -p '/tmp/:&$NiA'`), as well as various tricks involving passing strings through tokens/environment variables/input streams.(Citation: Bashfuscator Command Obfuscators)(Citation: FireEye Obfuscation June 2017) Adversaries may also use tricks such as directory traversals to obfuscate references to the binary being invoked by a command (`C:\voi\pcw\..\..\Windows\tei\qs\k\..\..\..\system32\erool\..\wbem\wg\je\..\..\wmic.exe shadowcopy delete`).(Citation: Twitter Richard WMIC) Tools such as Invoke-Obfuscation and Invoke-DOSfucation have also been used to obfuscate commands.(Citation: Invoke-DOSfuscation)(Citation: Invoke-Obfuscation)
T1027.010.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Behavior Prevention on Endpoint': On Windows 10+, enable Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules to block execution of potentially obfuscated scripts.
Mitigation 'Antivirus/Antimalware': Consider utilizing the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI) on Windows 10+ to analyze commands after being processed/interpreted.
T1027.010.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection Strategy for Command Obfuscation
- Analytic 'Analytic 1394': Detection of command-line activity exhibiting syntactic obfuscation patterns, such as excessive escape characters, base64 encoding, command concatenation, or outlier command length and entropy.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1395': Detection of shell commands that leverage encoded execution, command chaining, excessive piping, or unusual token patterns indicative of obfuscation.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1396': Detection of obfuscated commands via shell, osascript, or AppleScript interpreters using unusual tokens, encoding, variable substitution, or runtime string reconstruction.
T1027.010.procedures: Software 'Empire': Empire has the ability to obfuscate commands using Invoke-Obfuscation. Software 'PowerSploit': PowerSploit contains a collection of ScriptModification modules that compress and encode scripts and payloads. Software 'LoudMiner': LoudMiner has obfuscated various scripts. Software 'IceApple': IceApple can use Base64 and "junk" JavaScript code to obfuscate information. Software 'PowerPunch': PowerPunch can use Base64-encoded scripts. Software 'PHASEJAM': PHASEJAM has encoded commands with Base64. Software 'BADHATCH': BADHATCH malicious PowerShell commands can be encoded with base64. Software 'Denis': Denis has encoded its PowerShell commands in Base64. Software 'Sibot': Sibot has obfuscated scripts used in execution. Software 'ComRAT': ComRAT has used encryption and base64 to obfuscate its orchestrator code in the Registry. ComRAT has also used encoded PowerShell scripts. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can use obfuscated and encoded scripts. Software 'IronWind': IronWind has used Base64 encoding and XOR encryption with the key “53” to obfuscate command strings. Software 'Netwalker': Netwalker's PowerShell script has been obfuscated with multiple layers including base64 and hexadecimal encoding and XOR-encryption, as well as obfuscated PowerShell functions and variables. Software 'RogueRobin': The PowerShell script with the RogueRobin payload was obfuscated using the COMPRESS technique in `Invoke-Obfuscation`. Software 'CARROTBAT': CARROTBAT has the ability to execute obfuscated commands on the infected host. Software 'Emotet': Emotet has obfuscated macros within malicious documents to hide the URLs hosting the malware, CMD.exe arguments, and PowerShell scripts. Software 'FruitFly': FruitFly executes and stores obfuscated Perl scripts. Software 'Havoc': Havoc has utilized XOR encryption with the key “01-01-1900” to obfuscate command strings. Software 'Tsundere Botnet': Tsundere Botnet’s MSI installer has Base64-encoded command execution. Software 'SQLRat': SQLRat has used a character insertion obfuscation technique, making the script appear to contain Chinese characters. Software 'SHARPSTATS': SHARPSTATS has used base64 encoding and XOR to obfuscate PowerShell scripts. Software 'BackConfig': BackConfig has used compressed and decimal encoded VBS scripts. Software 'POWERSTATS': POWERSTATS uses character replacement, PowerShell environment variables, and XOR encoding to obfuscate code. POWERSTATS's backdoor code is a multi-layer obfuscated, encoded, and compressed blob. POWERSTATS has used PowerShell code with custom string obfuscation Software 'Zeus Panda': Zeus Panda obfuscates the macro commands in its initial payload. Software 'Astaroth': Astaroth has obfuscated and randomized parts of the JScript code it is initiating. Software 'CookieMiner': CookieMiner has used base64 encoding to obfuscate scripts on the system. Software 'KOCTOPUS': KOCTOPUS has obfuscated scripts with the BatchEncryption tool. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer has obfuscated scripts within text files used in execution. Software 'XORIndex Loader': XORIndex Loader has obfuscated strings using ASCII buffers and TextDecoder. Software 'Machete': Machete has used pyobfuscate, zlib compression, and base64 encoding for obfuscation. Machete has also used some visual obfuscation techniques by naming variables as combinations of letters to hinder analysis. Group 'Aquatic Panda': Aquatic Panda has encoded PowerShell commands in Base64. Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team has used ROT13 encoding, AES encryption and compression with the zlib library for their Python-based backdoor. Group 'HEXANE': HEXANE has used Base64-encoded scripts. Group 'Leafminer': Leafminer obfuscated scripts that were used on victim machines. Group 'Cobalt Group': Cobalt Group obfuscated several scriptlets and code used on the victim’s machine, including through use of XOR and RC4. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has encoded malicious PowerShell scripts using Base64. Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has obfuscated PowerShell scripts with Base64 encoding. Medusa Group has also obfuscated the code of dropped kernel drivers using a software known as Safengine Shielden which randomized the code through code mutations and then leveraged an embedded virtual machine interpreter to execute the code. Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has base64 encoded scripts to avoid detection. Group 'FIN6': FIN6 has used encoded PowerShell commands. Group 'APT32': APT32 has used the `Invoke-Obfuscation` framework to obfuscate their PowerShell. Group 'Play': Play has used Base64-encoded PowerShell scripts for post exploit activities on compromised hosts. Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound has used base64-encoded commands. Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider used Base64 encoding to obfuscate an Empire service and PowerShell commands. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used Daniel Bohannon’s Invoke-Obfuscation framework and obfuscated PowerShell scripts. The group has also used other obfuscation methods, including Base64 obfuscation of VBScripts and PowerShell commands. Group 'Patchwork': Patchwork has obfuscated a script with Crypto Obfuscator. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has encoded PowerShell commands. Group 'Silence': Silence has used environment variable string substitution for obfuscation. Group 'FIN8': FIN8 has used environment variables and standard input (stdin) to obfuscate command-line arguments. FIN8 also obfuscates malicious macros delivered as payloads. Group 'FIN7': FIN7 has used fragmented strings, environment variables, standard input (stdin), and native character-replacement functionalities to obfuscate commands. Group 'TA551': TA551 has used obfuscated variable names in a JavaScript configuration file. Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has used base64 encoding for scripts. Group 'APT19': APT19 used Base64 to obfuscate executed commands. Campaign 'Frankenstein': During Frankenstein, the threat actors ran encoded commands from the command line. Campaign 'C0021': During C0021, the threat actors used encoded PowerShell commands. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors executed an encoded VBScript file. Campaign 'C0018': During C0018, the threat actors used Base64 to encode their PowerShell scripts. Campaign 'SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation': During SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation, threat actors executed Base64-encoded PowerShell commands. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors executed PowerShell commands which were encoded or compressed using Base64, zlib, and XOR.

T1140.name: Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
T1140.description: Adversaries may use [Obfuscated Files or Information](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1027) to hide artifacts of an intrusion from analysis. They may require separate mechanisms to decode or deobfuscate that information depending on how they intend to use it. Methods for doing that include built-in functionality of malware or by using utilities present on the system. One such example is the use of [certutil](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0160) to decode a remote access tool portable executable file that has been hidden inside a certificate file.(Citation: Malwarebytes Targeted Attack against Saudi Arabia) Another example is using the Windows copy /b or type command to reassemble binary fragments into a malicious payload.(Citation: Carbon Black Obfuscation Sept 2016)(Citation: Sentinel One Tainted Love 2023) Sometimes a user's action may be required to open it for deobfuscation or decryption as part of [User Execution](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1204). The user may also be required to input a password to open a password protected compressed/encrypted file that was provided by the adversary.(Citation: Volexity PowerDuke November 2016)
T1140.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detect Adversary Deobfuscation or Decoding of Files and Payloads
- Analytic 'Analytic 0767': An adversary leverages built-in tools such as certutil.exe, powershell.exe, or copy.exe to decode, reassemble, or extract hidden malicious content from obfuscated containers or encoded formats. The decoding utility often spawns shortly after file staging or download and may be chained with script interpreters or further payload execution.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0768': The adversary uses native utilities like base64, gzip, tar, or openssl to decode, decompress, or decrypt files that were previously staged or downloaded. These tools may be chained with curl/wget and executed via bash/zsh, often to extract an embedded payload or reverse shell script.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0769': The adversary invokes built-in scripting or decoding tools like base64, plutil, or AppleScript-based utilities to decode files embedded in staging artifacts. Decoding often occurs post-download or as part of post-exploitation payload deployment via zsh, python, or osascript.
T1140.procedures: Software 'Imminent Monitor': Imminent Monitor has decoded malware components that are then dropped to the system. Software 'Expand': Expand can be used to decompress a local or remote CAB file into an executable. Software 'certutil': certutil has been used to decode binaries hidden inside certificate files as Base64 information. Software 'Brute Ratel C4': Brute Ratel C4 has the ability to deobfuscate its payload prior to execution. Software 'PcShare': PcShare has decrypted its strings by applying a XOR operation and a decompression using a custom implemented LZM algorithm. Software 'IronNetInjector': IronNetInjector has the ability to decrypt embedded .NET and PE payloads. Software 'PHPsert': PHPsert has the ability to decode and decrypt obfuscated strings prior to execution. Software 'SPAWNCHIMERA': SPAWNCHIMERA has decoded a XOR encoded private key. Software 'ZeroT': ZeroT shellcode decrypts and decompresses its RC4-encrypted payload. Software 'AppleJeus': AppleJeus has decoded files received from a C2. Software 'Action RAT': Action RAT can use Base64 to decode actor-controlled C2 server communications. Software 'KOCTOPUS': KOCTOPUS has deobfuscated itself before executing its commands. Software 'Snip3': Snip3 can decode its second-stage PowerShell script prior to execution. Software 'BendyBear': BendyBear has decrypted function blocks using a XOR key during runtime to evade detection. Software 'LiteDuke': LiteDuke has the ability to decrypt and decode multiple layers of obfuscation. Software 'P.A.S. Webshell': P.A.S. Webshell can use a decryption mechanism to process a user supplied password and allow execution. Software 'KONNI': KONNI has used certutil to download and decode base64 encoded strings and has also devoted a custom section to performing all the components of the deobfuscation process. Software 'Machete': Machete’s downloaded data is decrypted using AES. Software 'BOOSTWRITE': BOOSTWRITE has used a a 32-byte long multi-XOR key to decode data inside its payload. Software 'LockBit 3.0': The LockBit 3.0 payload is decrypted at runtime. Software 'RotaJakiro': RotaJakiro uses the AES algorithm, bit shifts in a function called `rotate`, and an XOR cipher to decrypt resources required for persistence, process guarding, and file locking. It also performs this same function on encrypted stack strings and the `head` and `key` sections in the network packet structure used for C2 communications. Software 'More_eggs': More_eggs will decode malware components that are then dropped to the system. Software 'DarkTortilla': DarkTortilla can decrypt its payload and associated configuration elements using the Rijndael cipher. Software 'Gelsemium': Gelsemium can decompress and decrypt DLLs and shellcode. Software 'ROAMINGHOUSE': ROAMINGHOUSE can decode and drop a malicious ZIP file prior to execution. Software 'Smoke Loader': Smoke Loader deobfuscates its code. Software 'Mispadu': Mispadu decrypts its encrypted configuration files prior to execution. Software 'Clop': Clop has used a simple XOR operation to decrypt strings. Software 'SDBbot': SDBbot has the ability to decrypt and decompress its payload to enable code execution. Software 'Babuk': Babuk has the ability to unpack itself into memory using XOR. Software 'BBSRAT': BBSRAT uses Expand to decompress a CAB file into executable content. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate can deobfuscate packed binaries in memory. Software 'Lokibot': Lokibot has decoded and decrypted its stages multiple times using hard-coded keys to deliver the final payload, and has decoded its server response hex string using XOR. Software 'POWERSTATS': POWERSTATS can deobfuscate the main backdoor code. Software 'QUADAGENT': QUADAGENT uses AES and a preshared key to decrypt the custom Base64 routine used to encode strings and scripts. Software 'VaporRage': VaporRage can deobfuscate XOR-encoded shellcode prior to execution. Software 'UPSTYLE': UPSTYLE encodes its main content prior to loading via Python as base64-encoded blobs. Software 'Lucifer': Lucifer can decrypt its C2 address upon execution. Software 'Caminho': Caminho can deobfuscate downloaded files prior to execution. Software 'Avaddon': Avaddon has decrypted encrypted strings. Software 'Line Dancer': Line Dancer shellcode payloads are base64 encoded when transmitted to compromised devices. Software 'SombRAT': SombRAT can run upload to decrypt and upload files from storage. Software 'XORIndex Loader': XORIndex Loader can decode its payload prior to execution. Software 'Mongall': Mongall has the ability to decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'WIREFIRE': WIREFIRE can decode, decrypt, and decompress data received in C2 HTTP `POST` requests. Software 'FoggyWeb': FoggyWeb can be decrypted in memory using a Lightweight Encryption Algorithm (LEA)-128 key and decoded using a XOR key. Software 'PHASEJAM': PHASEJAM has the ability to decode Base64 commands and data. Software 'RAPIDPULSE': RAPIDPULSE listens for specific HTTP query parameters in received communications. If specific parameters match, a hard-coded RC4 key is used to decrypt the HTTP query paremter hmacTime. This decrypts to a filename that is then open, read, encrypted with the same RC4 key, base64-encoded, written to standard out, then passed as a response to the HTTP request. Software 'Aria-body': Aria-body has the ability to decrypt the loader configuration and payload DLL. Software 'ShadowPad': ShadowPad has decrypted a binary blob to start execution. Software 'QUIETCANARY': QUIETCANARY can use a custom parsing routine to decode the command codes and additional parameters from the C2 before executing them. Software 'Starloader': Starloader decrypts and executes shellcode from a file called Stars.jps. Software 'TSCookie': TSCookie has the ability to decrypt, load, and execute a DLL and its resources. Software 'Conti': Conti has decrypted its payload using a hardcoded AES-256 key. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs decrypts and extracts a copy of its main DLL payload when executing. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay can decrypt its payload via a XOR key. Software 'DDKONG': DDKONG decodes an embedded configuration using XOR. Software 'Denis': Denis will decrypt important strings used for C&C communication. Software 'CASTLETAP': CASTLETAP can filter and deobfuscate an XOR encrypted activation string in the payload of an ICMP echo request. Software 'Bumblebee': Bumblebee can deobfuscate C2 server responses and unpack its code on targeted hosts. Software 'Final1stspy': Final1stspy uses Python code to deobfuscate base64-encoded strings. Software 'BoomBox': BoomBox can decrypt AES-encrypted files downloaded from C2. Software 'Chinoxy': The Chinoxy dropping function can initiate decryption of its config file. Software 'Azorult': Azorult uses an XOR key to decrypt content and uses Base64 to decode the C2 address. Software 'Astaroth': Astaroth uses a fromCharCode() deobfuscation method to avoid explicitly writing execution commands and to hide its code. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can deobfuscate and re-assemble code strings for execution. Software 'LunarMail': LunarMail can decrypt strings to retrieve configuration settings. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet decrypts resources that are loaded into memory and executed. Software 'Netwalker': Netwalker's PowerShell script can decode and decrypt multiple layers of obfuscation, leading to the Netwalker DLL being loaded into memory. Software 'Lumma Stealer': Lumma Stealer has used Base64-encoded content during execution, decoded via PowerShell. Software 'GlassWorm': GlassWorm has decoded its Base64 instructions. GlassWorm has also decrypted its AES protected payloads. Software 'NOOPLDR': NOOPLDR can decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'Squirrelwaffle': Squirrelwaffle has decrypted files and payloads using a XOR-based algorithm. Software 'AppleSeed': AppleSeed can decode its payload prior to execution. Software 'LP-Notes': LP-Notes has decrypted strings with lengths ranging from 15 to 19 characters using the same decryption key for each string. Software 'ABK': ABK has the ability to decrypt AES encrypted payloads. Software 'BRUSHFIRE': BRUSHFIRE has decrypted XOR strings prior to execution. Software 'OopsIE': OopsIE concatenates then decompresses multiple resources to load an embedded .Net Framework assembly. Software 'InvisibleFerret': InvisibleFerret has decoded XOR-encrypted and Base-64-encoded payloads prior to execution. Software 'ANELLDR': ANELLDR can decrypt encrypted payload data using AES-256-CBC and subsequently execute the payload in memory. Software 'KEYPLUG': KEYPLUG can decode its configuration file to determine C2 protocols. Software 'SystemBC': SystemBC has the ability to decrypt RC4 encrypted packets and to decode obfuscated data before C2 communication. Additionally, SystemBC has decrypted its config file that was encoded with XOR and a hardcoded 40-byte key. Software 'LIGHTWIRE': LIGHTWIRE can RC4 decrypt and Base64 decode C2 commands. Software 'Winnti for Linux': Winnti for Linux has decoded XOR encoded strings holding its configuration upon execution. Software 'CharmPower': CharmPower can decrypt downloaded modules prior to execution. Software 'Ebury': Ebury has verified C2 domain ownership by decrypting the TXT record using an embedded RSA public key. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can decrypt files and data. Software 'Bisonal': Bisonal has decoded strings in the malware using XOR and RC4. Software 'OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D': OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D uses a decode routine combining bit shifting and XOR operations with a variable key that depends on the length of the string that was encoded. If the computation for the variable XOR key turns out to be 0, the default XOR key of 0x1B is used. This routine is also referenced as the `rotate` function in reporting. Software 'PyDCrypt': PyDCrypt has decrypted and dropped the DCSrv payload to disk. Software 'Rising Sun': Rising Sun has decrypted itself using a single-byte XOR scheme. Additionally, Rising Sun can decrypt its configuration data at runtime. Software 'Crimson': Crimson can decode its encoded PE file prior to execution. Software 'Torisma': Torisma has used XOR and Base64 to decode C2 data. Software 'CorKLOG': CorKLOG has decoded XOR encrypted strings. Software 'Heyoka Backdoor': Heyoka Backdoor can decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'Chrommme': Chrommme can decrypt its encrypted internal code. Software 'Bankshot': Bankshot decodes embedded XOR strings. Software 'VERMIN': VERMIN decrypts code, strings, and commands to use once it's on the victim's machine. Software 'LightNeuron': LightNeuron has used AES and XOR to decrypt configuration files and commands. Software 'Kessel': Kessel has decrypted the binary's configuration once the main function was launched. Software 'GLASSTOKEN': GLASSTOKEN has the ability to decode hexadecimal and Base64 C2 requests. Software 'ISMInjector': ISMInjector uses the certutil command to decode a payload file. Software 'Sardonic': Sardonic can first decrypt with the RC4 algorithm using a hardcoded decryption key before decompressing. Software 'metaMain': metaMain can decrypt and load other modules. Software 'Exbyte': Exbyte decodes and decrypts data stored in the configuration file with a key provided on the command line during execution. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus has the ability to deobfuscate encrypted strings. Software 'Taidoor': Taidoor can use a stream cipher to decrypt stings used by the malware. Software 'PUBLOAD': PUBLOAD has decoded its payload prior to execution. Software 'CHIMNEYSWEEP': CHIMNEYSWEEP can use an embedded RC4 key to decrypt Windows API function strings. Software 'SampleCheck5000': SampleCheck5000 can decode and decrypt command line strings and files received through C2. Software 'HeartCrypt': HeartCrypt can decrypt payloads prior to execution. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb can decrypt strings related to communication configuration using RC4 with a static key. Software 'SLIGHTPULSE': SLIGHTPULSE can deobfuscate base64 encoded and RC4 encrypted C2 messages. Software 'RDAT': RDAT can deobfuscate the base64-encoded and AES-encrypted files downloaded from the C2 server. Software 'Cuckoo Stealer': Cuckoo Stealer strings are deobfuscated prior to execution. Software 'Egregor': Egregor has been decrypted before execution. Software 'DOWNIISSA': DOWNIISSA can decode strings prior to execution. Software 'RansomHub': RansomHub can use a provided passphrase to decrypt its configuration file. Software 'PureCrypter': PureCrypter can decrypt downloaded resources and parse internal files to determine its settings. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP deobfuscates embedded payloads. Software 'EnvyScout': EnvyScout can deobfuscate and write malicious ISO files to disk. Software 'Bundlore': Bundlore has used openssl to decrypt AES encrypted payload data. Bundlore has also used base64 and RC4 with a hardcoded key to deobfuscate data. Software 'RIFLESPINE': RIFLESPINE can deobfuscate encrypted files prior to execution on targeted hosts. Software 'MOPSLED': MOPSLED can decrypt obfuscated configuration files. Software 'HyperBro': HyperBro can unpack and decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'Dyre': Dyre decrypts resources needed for targeting the victim. Software 'Waterbear': Waterbear has the ability to decrypt its RC4 encrypted payload for execution. Software 'Ursnif': Ursnif has used crypto key information stored in the Registry to decrypt Tor clients dropped to disk. Software 'Exaramel for Linux': Exaramel for Linux can decrypt its configuration file. Software 'ROADSWEEP': ROADSWEEP can decrypt embedded scripts prior to execution. Software 'Avenger': Avenger has the ability to decrypt files downloaded from C2. Software 'AvosLocker': AvosLocker has deobfuscated XOR-encoded strings. Software 'YAHOYAH': YAHOYAH decrypts downloaded files before execution. Software 'LockBit 2.0': LockBit 2.0 can decode scripts and strings in loaded modules. Software 'IceApple': IceApple can use a Base64-encoded AES key to decrypt tasking. Software 'BBK': BBK has the ability to decrypt AES encrypted payloads. Software 'TYPEFRAME': One TYPEFRAME variant decrypts an archive using an RC4 key, then decompresses and installs the decrypted malicious DLL module. Another variant decodes the embedded file by XORing it with the value "0x35". Software 'Ninja': The Ninja loader component can decrypt and decompress the payload. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has decoded XOR encrypted strings prior to execution in memory. Software 'Sagerunex': Sagerunex uses a custom decryption routine to unpack itself during installation. Software 'LunarLoader': LunarLoader can deobfuscate files containing the next stages in the infection chain. Software 'BRICKSTORM': BRICKSTORM has decoded its encrypted C2 traffic prior to execution. BRICKSTORM also has the ability to decode its obfuscated payload before execution. Software 'AshTag': The AshTag stager compoment can decode and decrypt Base64 and XOR-encrypted payloads. Software 'BOOKWORM': BOOKWORM has decoded its Base64 encoded payload prior to execution. BOOKWORM has also encrypted files with RC4 and has decrypted its payload prior to execution. Software 'Pteranodon': Pteranodon can decrypt encrypted data strings prior to using them. Software 'LAMEHUG': LAMEHUG can decode and drop a decoy file attached to spearphishing emails. Software 'Mori': Mori can resolve networking APIs from strings that are ADD-encrypted. Software 'Embargo': Embargo has utilized MDeployer to decrypt two payloads that contain MS4Killer toolkit b.cache and the Embargo ransomware executable a.cache with a hardcoded RC4 key `wlQYLoPCil3niI7x8CvR9EtNtL/aeaHrZ23LP3fAsJogVTIzdnZ5Pi09ZVeHFkiB`. Software 'Emotet': Emotet has used a self-extracting RAR file to deliver modules to victims. Emotet has also extracted embedded executables from files using hard-coded buffer offsets. Software 'WarzoneRAT': WarzoneRAT can use XOR 0x45 to decrypt obfuscated code. Software 'Green Lambert': Green Lambert can use multiple custom routines to decrypt strings prior to execution. Software 'Proton': Proton uses an encrypted file to store commands and configuration values. Software 'Gootloader': Gootloader has the ability to decode and decrypt malicious payloads prior to execution. Software 'INC Ransomware': INC Ransomware can run `CryptStringToBinaryA` to decrypt base64 content containing its ransom note. Software 'Kobalos': Kobalos decrypts strings right after the initial communication, but before the authentication process. Software 'BabyShark': BabyShark has the ability to decode downloaded files prior to execution. Software 'Carbon': Carbon decrypts task and configuration files for execution. Software 'HermeticWiper': HermeticWiper can decompress and copy driver files using `LZCopy`. Software 'Remexi': Remexi decrypts the configuration data using XOR with 25-character keys. Software 'gh0st RAT': gh0st RAT has decrypted and loaded the gh0st RAT DLL into memory, once the initial dropper executable is launched. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can deobfuscate Base64-encoded strings and scripts. Software 'OSX/Shlayer': OSX/Shlayer can base64-decode and AES-decrypt downloaded payloads. Software 'TONESHELL': TONESHELL has decoded its payload prior to execution. Software 'CookieMiner': CookieMiner has used Google Chrome's decryption and extraction operations. Software 'REPTILE': The REPTILE launcher component can decrypt kernel module code from a file and load it into memory. Software 'DropBook': DropBook can unarchive data downloaded from the C2 to obtain the payload and persistence modules. Software 'RegDuke': RegDuke can decrypt strings with a key either stored in the Registry or hardcoded in the code. Software 'Shamoon': Shamoon decrypts ciphertext using an XOR cipher and a base64-encoded string. Software 'DUSTPAN': DUSTPAN decodes and decrypts embedded payloads. Software 'HUI Loader': HUI Loader can decrypt and load files containing malicious payloads. Software 'Skidmap': Skidmap has the ability to download, unpack, and decrypt tar.gz files . Software 'ROKRAT': ROKRAT can decrypt strings using the victim's hostname as the key. Software 'Siloscape': Siloscape has decrypted the password of the C2 server with a simple byte by byte XOR. Software 'MESSAGETAP': After checking for the existence of two files, keyword_parm.txt and parm.txt, MESSAGETAP XOR decodes and read the contents of the files. Software 'ShimRat': ShimRat has decompressed its core DLL using shellcode once an impersonated antivirus component was running on a system. Software 'Drovorub': Drovorub has de-obsfuscated XOR encrypted payloads in WebSocket messages. Software 'GoldMax': GoldMax has decoded and decrypted the configuration file when executed. Software 'MegaCortex': MegaCortex has used a Base64 key to decode its components. Software 'Lizar': Lizar has decrypted its configuration data, such as the C2 IP address, ports and other network communication. Software 'Turian': Turian has the ability to use a XOR decryption key to extract C2 server domains and IP addresses. Software 'SideTwist': SideTwist can decode and decrypt messages received from C2. Software 'REvil': REvil can decode encrypted strings to enable execution of commands and payloads. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer has decoded its payload prior to execution. Software 'THINCRUST': THINCRUST can deobfuscate RSA encrypted C2 commands received through the DEVICEID cookie. Software 'Agent Tesla': Agent Tesla has the ability to decrypt strings encrypted with the Rijndael symmetric encryption algorithm. Software 'WellMail': WellMail can decompress scripts received from C2. Software 'CoinTicker': CoinTicker decodes the initially-downloaded hidden encoded file using OpenSSL. Software 'DarkWatchman': DarkWatchman has the ability to self-extract as a RAR archive. Software 'SharpStage': SharpStage has decompressed data received from the C2 server. Software 'DarkGate': DarkGate installation includes binary code stored in a file located in a hidden directory, such as shell.txt, that is decrypted then executed. Software 'MuddyViper': MuddyViper has decrypted the embedded HackBrowserData tool prior to execution. Software 'DEADWOOD': DEADWOOD XORs some strings within the binary using the value 0xD5, and deobfuscates these items at runtime. Software 'Sibot': Sibot can decrypt data received from a C2 and save to a file. Software 'SplatDropper': SplatDropper has decoded XOR encrypted payload. Software 'Pillowmint': Pillowmint has been decompressed by included shellcode prior to being launched. Software 'Hildegard': Hildegard has decrypted ELF files with AES. Software 'IronWind': IronWind can deobfuscate the next stage payload using Base64 and XOR operations with the key "53". Software 'ComRAT': ComRAT has used unique per machine passwords to decrypt the orchestrator payload and a hardcoded XOR key to decrypt its communications module. Software 'Clambling': Clambling can deobfuscate its payload prior to execution. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot decodes the configuration data and modules. Software 'FIVEHANDS': FIVEHANDS has the ability to decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'Spark': Spark has used a custom XOR algorithm to decrypt the payload. Software 'PowGoop': PowGoop can decrypt PowerShell scripts for execution. Software 'StrelaStealer': StrelaStealer payloads have included strings encrypted via XOR. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used malware to decrypt encrypted CAB files. Group 'WIRTE': WIRTE has used Base64 to decode malicious VBS script. Group 'Storm-1811': Storm-1811 has distributed password-protected archives such as ZIP files during intrusions. Group 'ZIRCONIUM': ZIRCONIUM has used the AES256 algorithm with a SHA1 derived key to decrypt exploit code. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has decoded base64-encoded PowerShell, JavaScript, and VBScript. Group 'OilRig': A OilRig macro has run a PowerShell command to decode file contents.
